### ealachan — 2012-11-18T17:48:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65147>

Took it easy all day yesterday - mostly sat around knitting and watching movies with the hubs, which was nice. Back is still a little sore, but not can't-move-at-all sore like it was Friday night and yesterday morning. Ibuprofen and ice has been helping.

Darleen - I've never been to a chiro, no. The idea kind of freaks me out a little, although I know they help a lot of people. It just seems like once you start going to one, you have to keep going, you know? Some of the ones around here seem to operate like that, anyway.

Got all my T-day shopping done this morning, so that's out of the way, thankfully! Hubs is working late on Wednesday night, so it should work out pretty well, as I'll be able to do all my prep ahead of time without feeling bad for spending all evening in the kitchen.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-18T18:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65157>

Re the chiro ... no you do NOT have to "keep going" any more than you have to keep going to the massage therapist. It's a good idea to get an occasional tune up but not necessary.

Our chiro (who is so danged busy it's a 6 week wait to get in to see him) also has a masters in sports medicine. He is FANTASTIC. He's so good and so well educated that DH's doc at the VA gave him permission to continue to treat Joe after his back and then neck surgery.

BTW it was the chiro who insisted that they do an MRI on Joe after the neck surgery. He had started having headaches which began right after the surgery and became so bad that he couldn't get out of bed AT ALL. The doc at the VA had decided he just had a sinus infection. I was at my wit's end because you know, men do not listen to their women. We don't know anything.

They scheduled the MRI for that day and found he had a hematoma that filled 1/4 of his skull. I am not kidding. I saw the images. The upper left quadrant of his head was full of blood. Good thing it was a slow bleed or he'd have died.

Anyway. Jim, our chiro, is the absolute BEST in my book. Besides saving DH's life, he helped his shoulder pain which they finally figured out was caused by the way he (hubby) was hanging off the train cars at work. He'd thought he was going to need surgery but Jim fixed it.  Jim uses a lot of additional therapies including deep tissue massage (OW!!!), heat, muscle stimulation with some kinda hoodoo electrical thingy (that's the technical term for it, btw). And when he's done he tells you to go home and ICE whatever was bothering you.

So ask around. I'd think you'd have a good chiro somewhere in the vicinity. We swear by 'em.

### LinD — 2012-11-18T19:51:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65173>

I didn't believe in chiros until back in late 2004/early 2005, I ended up with a C4/C5 issue (herniation). The therapy at the orthopedic office was not helping, and I chickened out on the cortisone injection. So I asked for prayer in Sunday school class when a fellow member told me his story. So I proceeded that route and after 5 weeks of therapy (3 days per), I was in tons better shape. Also, I recommend a book by McKenzie, "7 Steps to a Pain-Free Life: How to Rapidly Relieve Back and Neck Pain."

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/0452282772](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/0452282772)

I still do the exercises occasionally, plus pay attention to my neck position all the time. I'm sure there is the same issue with backs.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T05:21:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65266>

Since it's your doing that I am now pretty consistently doing CT on a daily basis I thought I'd ask YOU .... are you just doing cold water or have you also added ice? I've been going over the protocol post again because I was thinking that after my 2nd week of playing ice queen I'd need to actually add some ice. Since I can do the big bag of ice on my bare belly when I'm not IN the tub I'm hoping it will be fine when I'm actually in the cold water. I hope.

### ealachan — 2012-11-19T17:19:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65350>

> **DarleenMB;67273 wrote:** Since it's your doing that I am now pretty consistently doing CT on a daily basis I thought I'd ask YOU .... are you just doing cold water or have you also added ice? I've been going over the protocol post again because I was thinking that after my 2nd week of playing ice queen I'd need to actually add some ice. Since I can do the big bag of ice on my bare belly when I'm not IN the tub I'm hoping it will be fine when I'm actually in the cold water. I hope.

I don't do ice because I'm a lazy \*\*\*\*, basically...LOL I don't see the point of schlepping re-usable ice containers back and forth every time for just a few extra degrees' difference, and I'm certainly not going to shell out for pre-made ice every time I want to take a bath. My tap water is currently coming out at about 52 degrees, and my last bath in that got my skin down pretty close to the "magic" 55 degree mark after 30 minutes, so I'm ok with it. I am hesitant to go super-hardcore-cold because I'd rather detox more gradually, at least for now. I have like 30 years' worth of bad \*\*\*\* floating around in my fat and I don't really want it all hitting my system in a short amount of time, since I have to actually function and work 40 hours a week and stuff. If I could take a couple months off and do nothing but CT and detox, then I'd be hitting it as hard as I could...but I have to balance this whole craziness with an actual LIFE, you know?

### ealachan — 2012-11-19T17:41:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65357>

**EP Journal, Day 247**

_In which our heroine's back STILL \*\*\*\*ing hurts, ugh!_

b: 3 strips bacon, 6-ish oz venison, two eggs, coffee w/ butter, hwc, cocoa

l: probably not going to need any

d: not sure yet

\- skipped dinner last night because we had a late lunch of sausage and spinach crustless quiche (blog post coming on that...it was wicked yummy) and neither of us were hungry by dinner time. Did have one beer, some dark choc and some walnuts around 6pm.

\- had quite a lot of oxytocin yesterday evening...heh. Went to bed at 10, fell asleep pretty quickly, slept fine until it started to get light out, then I woke up and couldn't get all the way back to sleep. Got up and walked the pup at 7am - it was 22 degrees out. Was out in tshirt, yoga pants, hat and mittens for about 15 min or so.

\- back STILL hurts. It seemed fine most of yesterday. I don't know if the semi-energetic boinking last night caused it to regress or what, but it's back to me not really being able to lean, twist or reach to the left much without causing pain. Also, I think I figured out what caused it. Friday afternoon at work, the water cooler was empty and I hoisted a full 5-gallon water jug onto the machine to refill it. Back didn't hurt right then and there, but it definitely started hurting soon after, and left was the direction I had to turn to get the bottle onto the machine. I've done that same lift and movement before and not had an issue, but maybe I was just postioned slightly differently or something this last time. Joy.

\- Adding insult to literal injury, I woke up with a tiny zit right on the end of my nose.  I'm trying to leave it alone. Trying. Not entirely succeeding.

\- fed my husband venison for breakfast. I didn't tell him what it was until it was cooked and on the plate, so he couldn't really argue. He didn't NOT eat it, so I guess it wasn't too offensive to him... He's a city boy, and stuff like wild game makes him recoil a little bit, but I grew up eating venison and really like it once in a while.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T18:24:01+0200

sorry your back is still bothering you. There's also the alternative of seeing a massage therapist or maybe even an acupuncturist. I know it can be expensive but if that's what you need to fix it then my suggestion (unasked for I know) would be to fix it.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-19T19:14:21+0200

We have a stash of vicodin from various things. Happy to mail you some! I try to remain open minded about chiro/massage as a therapy. The rate of resolution of back pain due to a specific injury without treatment and with treatment are the same.

Seems like those that are of the mind to go to see a chiro/massage person, also seem to be the type to benefit from the placebo effect and spiritual related well-being.

So I try those, yet come back to my original conclusion - that for me, since I'm missing the spiritual/placebo/benefit from the bonding kind of person -it's not gonna help.

You are probably somewhere in between I suspect - but I totally get the gist of not wanting to do either due to fat. I was exact same way.

### ealachan — 2012-11-19T19:39:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65385>

I have a stash of vicodin too, but I'm always so afraid of it making me barf that I hardly ever end up taking it. I actually have a pretty high pain tolerance for the most part, and I try not to take medication unless I really need it. That having been said, hubs' Rx 800mg ibuprofens were my best friend all weekend. Wish I'd have remembered to take one before I left for work this morning!

The thing about chiros that really scares me is this: All it takes is one snap to throw something even MORE out of whack. My dad has sworn by his chiro for years, but I've always seen him come back from there limping just as much as when he went in, and taking like three days to get back to feeling good anyway. That, to me, seems not really worth it. Also, I know several people whose chiros have them on a "schedule". They go in every 3 months (or every month, in one case) to get "adjusted", whether anything is out of whack or not. That kind of thing always makes me a little suspicious. I know not all chiros operate like that of course, but it just sends up kind of a red flag for me in general.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-19T19:50:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65388>

I like rolfing (or maybe I should say, I like the effects). The process is less than fun but it helps me a lot (haha - I first typed that as "hurts a lot." Freudian slip). Some rolers may be trained to do high velocity moves (e.g. the "cracking" that chiros do) but I wouldn't let someone do it unless they do it a lot. Like many times a day. Neck adjustments cause strokes at a rate of 1 in a million, which doesn't sound bad until you're that one (as was a friend's sister). Hope your back gets better - I know how much that ruins my mood.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T20:32:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65394>

I can verify, and so can hubby, that there is no placebo effect going on. He came home from work one time and he looked exactly like the crooked man. It took about 3 visits to the chiro to get him straightened out.

My first visit was because I had developed a horrible pain in my lower back right near my hip. THe doc couldn't figure out what was wrong and the nurse told me (after he'd left) to go see the chiro. He got rid of the pain and he was (and still is) the only one who can fix my neck. I will suffer for weeks, sometimes months, it never corrects itself. I don't know what it is that he does but it works.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-19T21:14:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65401>

> **DarleenMB;67412 wrote:** I can verify, and so can hubby, that there is no placebo effect going on. He came home from work one time and he looked exactly like the crooked man. It took about 3 visits to the chiro to get him straightened out.
>
>
>
> My first visit was because I had developed a horrible pain in my lower back right near my hip. THe doc couldn't figure out what was wrong and the nurse told me (after he'd left) to go see the chiro. He got rid of the pain and he was (and still is) the only one who can fix my neck. I will suffer for weeks, sometimes months, it never corrects itself. I don't know what it is that he does but it works.

When I say "placebo effect", I don't mean it's imaginary and subsequently full of bollocks. I am not putting it down! The great part about it (they should rename it now that we know more about it) is that in the correct setting, with the correct expectations - "placebo" inclined patients can actually generate physical changes that improve their condition. The demeanor of the practitioner is key - I think it's related to that weird "catching the mood of others positive thing". (on that note, I sorry if you catch my mood today!). It isn't that people simply PERCEIVE changes, they actually have concrete and observable changes in a significant way compared to a double blind control.

I need a new word beside placebo - I think people have a pre-idea that it means "imaginary", which is not what I mean.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T21:34:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65412>

Ok, gotcha. And I know exactly what you mean. It's what Jack has been trying to get across to everyone for months now.

Our minds are incredibly powerful (and I'm talking mind, not brain). What we think can become reality. change your thinking, change your life and all that stuff. And yes, I do believe in that.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-19T21:45:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65416>

>
>
> Our minds are incredibly powerful (and I'm talking mind, not brain). What we think can become reality. change your thinking, change your life and all that stuff. And yes, I do believe in that.

I didn't think of it like that - but what you say sounds spot on. I disagree in one big way with Jack on this though, I don't think it is something that applies across the board to all people. I think this ability is part of the hand we are dealt when our genetic deck is shuffled, and constantly telling the crabby, unenlightened, hopeless and scroogesqe folks to "just meditate", "just give it up to Jesus" is the same as the lifelong athlete and gymrat telling the super obese "you just need to push away from the table and jazzercise".

### ealachan — 2012-11-19T22:34:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ealachans-leptin-reset-saga.155/post-65431>

I have to agree with this, to a certain extent. It just means you have to keep digging to find what DOES work for you. There are no silver bullets or golden arrows or platinum...spears? Whatever. You know what I mean. One size shouldn't fit all.

> I can't get into a cold shower. It just doesn't work. I normally only take tepid showers anyway, so I can get into my normal-temp shower and then slowly work the temp down as I go along, but trying to make myself step into an already-cold shower spray? No can do. Point being - if you can stand cold showers, I bet you could do a cold bath pretty easily! It only really sucks for the first few seconds when you first get in, but then it's fine...for me, at least. I kind of lower myself down into the water in stages, because I'm afraid if I get in all at once I'll give myself a heart attack...LOL! I sit on the edge of the tub with my lower legs in, then slowly lower my butt down into the water with knees still bent, then stretch out one leg so it's totally submerged, then do the other one. I swear a lot during the whole process, too. That definitely helps.  Usually I bring my iPod in with me and play Bejeweled while I'm in there, but last time I actually brought a book and the time went faster, once I was able to concentrate on the words.

Can I please ask what goggles you are using?

Cal


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Not looking to lose weight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### canuck — 2012-03-15T18:09:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-857>

Hey Everyone,

I am not looking to lose weight, but to balance out my hormones and get some energy back. 14 months of amenorrhea, overtraining has actually caused a weight loss (but increased bf % due to muscle loss). Low progesterone, am on prometrium, also have a prescription for estrogen but dont want to take it. My question is, should I be stuffing my face with calories or follow my appetite and possibly be around the 1000-1300 cal mark. I am 5'3 and 115lbs, i am up from an all time low of 108 which terrified me so i ate a lot of fruit and paleo carbs, probably 3-4000 calories some days. Any similiar stories or advice out there?

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-16T02:52:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-1203>

Canuk, I'm right there with you. I'm 24, 5'3" and 113 lbs. I finished a round of hcg in Sept. at 120, dropped to 117 on the leptin reset (keto-paleo style) and since adding CT in February, I've dropped four more. The thing is I'm dropping it in places I don't need to and the love handles remain! Clearly, this is an indication of hormonal issues. I have adrenal fatigue and hashimotos that I'm struggling with.

I stick to 1200-1400 calories most days, but then I can pound it back other days, easily reaching that 3,000 cal mark. I would also like advice on this topic. I really need my body to detox extrogen (got off birth control in Feb. too) and get a handle on my low thyroid issues.

Let's continue to exchange notes. I appreciate your post on this. I think just listening to your body and not denying it food when it needs it is key...but it's hard with decreased appetite as a result of CT.

### canuck — 2012-03-16T16:49:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-1473>

May I ask why you did the HCG diet? Seems like you didnt need to lose weight, was it an attempt at balancing hormones?

I have low DHEA but still within range that my doctors dont see it as a problem (go figure). I am in Canada and you cannot buy DHEA without a prescription, but I have a small supply that I snuck across the boarder but my naturopath said we should get my progesterone numbers up first. Took me 6 months to get an appt with an endocrinologist and her advice was for me to gain 10lbs, but she also gave me a 6 month supply of prometrium and estrogen (I am not taking the estrogen). I know its not a weight thing since I lost my period when I was my heaviest about 127, but was probably my most muscular at this weight as well. I also have a lot of digestive issues and since everything is controlled via hormones, its probably the source of most of problems (again docs are not convinced of that connection). I too am seeing weight loss in the wrong places and have seen more weight around the love handles.

Trying everything, getting colonics to help with constipation, which has helped quite a bit. Osteopath for getting colon functioning which is a new thing but my osteopath is a paleo supporter and has noticed a few lesions around my descending colon which we are working out and acupuncture. I am also going to start seeing a psychologist. I dont feel overly stressed or depressed but might as well see if it works. Glad I have good benefits.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-16T17:54:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-1532>

Canuk, I've struggled with my weight my whole life. My highest weight was 177.7. I got down to 138 for my wedding in 2010, but within a month or two I had gained 10 lbs. back. I was fat and frustrated, and I ran into hcg in the health store. I gave it a whirl, and the rest is history. I believe if I hadn't found hcg, I wouldn't have found paleo and then Dr. K. So I'm very grateful.

I'm down another pound this morning to 112.4, and it's starting to freak me out. I have NEVER before had to worry about losing too much weight. I have ALWAYS been the fat girl. I plan to continue keto-paleo, but I'm going to increase cals and see how it goes.

Sounds like we are struggling with the same battles. For constipation, I take Natural Calm with Calcium in the evenings. I also have added DE to the mix, which has really helped. Betaine hcl helps me digest all the proteins I'm taking in, and I eat plenty of CO, too, which helps.

Have you tried Dr. K's leaky gut protocol? In addition to thyroid and adrenal issues, I have leaky gut and candida (I'm guessing), so I follow a pretty strict diet. I haven't been tested for DHEA, but I plan to get a full panel done when I see a doc on March 28. Currently, I'm on a low dose of cytomel, t3 therapy, which has seemed to help. Granted, I started it at the same time as CT, so who knows what is actually working?

I hope the psychologist helps. Sometimes the emotional burdens we carry are as toxic as the yucky stuff we carry inside.

I really want to balance out my figure, regain my period and get my hormones in check. I'm praying CT is the answer.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-17T01:25:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-1765>

Canuk, I had one of my 3,000 calorie days. It's amazing how I go from being not hungry at all to ravenous! The weight loss is starting to freak me out though, so even though I'm pretty full, I'm taking the urge to eat as a good thing right now.

Dr. K says if you're hungry while doing CT, your hormones are out of whack though...so who knows what's good and bad?

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-17T01:26:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-1766>

Wait, I forgot to ask, are you following the leptin rx and ct?

### chewingthefat — 2012-03-18T00:22:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-2152>

I have this problem as well. I don't want to lose any more weight and have limited my CT as a result, but really want to delve into it full force. My hormonal issues emerge rapidly when I have too low body fat. I wonder if Jack has found any differences in the way women react to CT as opposed to men?

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-19T01:44:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-looking-to-lose-weight.158/post-2513>

### hellojtm — 2012-04-12T03:26:43+0300

Preferrably they would already be lean so that we can figure out if they are just becoming even leaner or if they are losing muscle by eating to satiety. I'm in the same boat as you all except I'm male. I lost 4lbs doing CT and for the most part I believe it was body fat even thought I was already lean ~12%, I'm ok as long as I don't go below like 7% body fat and for women I'm assuming you're looking at maybe 13-14%... I hadn't read that hunger during CT implies hormone problems, but it \*kinda\* makes sense. For me I have been using it (the fact that I'm hungry) as an indication that I'm not cold adapted yet. But now that is getting kind of silly as I've started to exhibit all the outwards signs of being cold adapted like ... cold tolerance ... darker skin.

jesse

# Thread: low carb= loose stools for me
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sandy — 2012-03-15T18:16:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-864>

I am always amazed at people who get constipated on this diet.

for me, its the total opposite.

yesterday was my first day, and already this morning the loose stools have started. within an hour of eating

warning, this will get graphic

in the past, I have had it so bad, its almost like "peeing out of my butt"

it comes on with no warning, so if I am not near a bathroom, I am in real trouble.

sometimes, its so frequent that it happens 5 times an hour and I get sore from going so much

I eat mainly meat. veggies are just there to help the meat go down. I eat some dairy(but not yesterday or today). I eat plenty of fat

So, am I not digesting the meat or fat? am I overdigesting it? If I am not digesting it, shouldn't I be losing weight like crazy?

ox bile, HCL, digestive enzymes?

I would really be interesting in hearing from someone who has had this problem and how you resolved it

### Dineen — 2012-03-15T18:55:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-885>

Going paleo completely relieved a lifetime of constipation for me. From things I've read, people who get constipated on a low carb diet are those who were "addicted" to fiber. You may have a corrollary issue with fiber, needing it to "bulk up" or slow down your digestion in the large bowel.

Your particular 5 times an hour and very liquid stool, may be other issues with side effects of over supplementation (frequent issues with too much glutamine, Vitamin C, or magnesium, for instance). That was the past, so stick with what's going on now.

Fat also is a great help for those with constipation issues, so you may want to adjust your fat consumption down just a bit.

You can learn a lot about what normal stool patterns are from gutsense.org. There is a great image of the Bristol stool scale with explanations of normal there.

Liquid is not good. Soft is good; daily and even after every meal is good.

I find that if I eat too many fibrous veggies (more than a cup of grean beans at a time, for instance) I don't have a daily bowel movement and feel bloated or "backed up". My stools are still soft to pass though. So you may want to eat more veggies and more fibrous ones rather than just lettuce, for instance.

### sandy — 2012-03-15T19:05:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-892>

thanks

I don't take vit C, the loose stools happened before I took mag(and I take a chelated form), only take 1 glutamine, but not consistent with that, although the loose stools are.

I think more veggies would help, but, one, they increase my cravings, and two, I get the feeling Dr K does not think we should be eating many veggies(esp during the winter) and that we should try to eat Ketogenic and as low carb as possible

### Croak — 2012-03-15T19:27:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-909>

Start with some HCL, and realize that you need time to adapt to high fat/high protein intake and the loss of carbs. Could take weeks, and loose movements will be something you'll be dealing with for some time. And there's usually other factors in play (toxin release, overgrowth die-off) that will keep you paying attention to the quality of your toilet tissue.

It eventually settles down.

### Phoenix — 2012-03-18T00:28:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-2153>

Even though your problem started before you started taking magnesium, I'd still cut it out. And cut out all supplements for that matter. Also remove dairy and cut back on fat and remove nightshades. Remove all starches too. Be especially careful that no gluten gets into your diet.

Then see where that leaves you after a week or so. If your diarrhea is better, then start adding back one by one to find what the problem was. Wait at least a week between each addition. If it isn't better, then go the route of adding in bile or HCL.

Not telling you what to do ... that's just what I would do.

Good luck! Hope you figure it out!

### agatha — 2012-03-26T23:51:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-6603>

Hi Sandy

Have you tried probiotics - could be a gut flora problem. Or it could be that you are experiencing a detox reaction - the liver dumps fat soluble toxins into the bile which then creates diarrhoea further down the gut. If you are unlucky, your gut will then reabsorb some of the toxins and so the cycle repeats itself. I have had success with low carb diarrhoea using activated charcoal after breakfast. Don't do this for more than a few days though - the charcoal will absorb some of the nutrients.

Hope you get it fixed.

I'm not eating any fruit, in addition to it not being on the leptin RS, but also because I suspect fructose malaborption also.

I take Now Super Enzymes (5 capsules) with every meal.

It is a slow and frustrating process.

Something like the SCD diet or GAPS might be helpful also. I am going completely dairy-free now and adding in small amounts of easily digestible veggies to see if it helps my issue. My next step will be to stop all supplementation and see what happens. I take an adrenal supplement, magnesium, Vitamin D, Vitamin K, Vitamin C, B Vitamins and glutamine.

If you'd like to PM back and forth, please let me know. I could use support on this issue, too. It is tough to stick with something that feels like it is making my digestion worse. But I am trying to keep in mind that in the long run, it will be better. I can feel the health effects already, so I know that it is a good change.

### Glamazon — 2012-04-01T20:28:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-9877>

I do so without adding any supplements. I do drinks a lot of water to compensate for the liquid stools. I get very thirsty during that time. Yep, it is like I am peeing out my butt.

### Owl — 2012-04-01T20:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-loose-stools-for-me.159/post-9889>

I have this same problem. It started when I went on the hcg diet a few years ago. I was eating no grains or starches, and 500 calories per day. I would get loose stools/diarrhea while I was on the diet, and it would clear up when I added grains and starches back in.

After my last round, I had trouble keeping the weight off, so I kept with the low carbs and also kept the loose stools. Probiotics didn't do anything for me.

I thought that's how it was going to be, but since going paleo, I find that other people actually have constipation problems!

I just did the GI Effects stool test and some other blood work. No bad bacteria, no candida, no h.pylori or anything like that. Most of my good bacteria levels are good. I do have a non-human parasite. But they say it should be transient and resolve on it's own and usually doesn't cause many symptoms, and to work on other problems before trying to eradicate that. But that's something you might investigate yourself. I don't think the parasite is my problem, since it's been going on for 2 years and seems to correlate with starchy carb intake.

Anyway, I've decided to switch my chelated magnesium malate, which was causing my stools to be looser, to chelated mag glycinate. I bought this brand:[http://www.amazon.com/Albion-Chelated-Magnesium-200mg-VegCap/dp/B002TU58QA](http://http://www.amazon.com/Albion-Chelated-Magnesium-200mg-VegCap/dp/B002TU58QA)

I've been taking it for a week, and have worked up to 1000mg/day. These are loose powder in capsules, unlike most magnesium which is pressed pills. It seems to be much better than the malate on my bowels. I think I'm absorbing it better before it has a chance to get to my bowels.

For the past few days, I have also been taking Now brand Betaine HCL with pepsin. I'm up to 4 capsules/meal. I don't know if this will help my stools, but it's worth a try.

# Thread: An ER Doc from California
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 69
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RobH — 2012-03-15T21:29:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-1007>

I'm an ER doc living in Northern California. Have plenty of weight to lose myself, and have struggled with it for many many years -- lots of exercise, nearly every dieting trick out there. Have lost it and gained it back mostly. Got a great heart rate monitor app for my iPhone and have done like 89 workouts in the last 4 months, burned several hundred thousand calories, tried to eat "healthy" following the Zone type diet, all while losing about 6 pounds.

Discovered (why the heck did it take me so long? Robb Wolf was just down the road in Chico) a month and a half ago, and Jack Kruse 2 weeks or while watching the Paleo Summit. Haven't felt better in 10 years since I started Paleo and now the Leptin Rx and CT (cold swimming pool this time of year) and the weight is melting off -- 23 pounds so far. Love it and can't wait for my next cold soak. I have not felt this good or this optimistic about ANYTHING in years. I have three little boys and a wonderful wife and want to be the healthiest fittest person (OPTIMAL!) I can for them and everybody in my life.

Also so important to me: Every day at work I take care of so many people suffering from the effects of chronic low-level inflammation and it is frankly mindblowing -- so many young people disabled from chronic pain, obesity, even MIs (heart attacks) in 30 years olds. So many elderly and middle aged people so tragically decrepit from every neolithic disease. So much suffering from what is so preventable and so many people living with it. I've started talking to patients about gluten free diets, recommending paleo books and really trying to hone in on interventions like that that I think will be helpful -- a little odd for an ER doc to be doing. I've read incessantly on Paleo diets and looked at some of the research -- and I am very convinced of the validity of the approach (as I'm sure you all are) but I had to be on sound footing to recommend it to my patients.

Jack, I cannot thank you enough for what you are doing. I can't wait to see your PaleoFX speech and hope to help you change the way we deliver HEALTHcare (not disease care) going forward. I know you're doing it not for the awards, but I'm thinking Nobel Prize in Medicine...

Thanks so much for what you are doing and how you are doing it.

\-RobH

### Birdy — 2012-03-15T21:31:54+0200

Hi Doc!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

### Paradox — 2012-03-15T21:57:04+0200

Welcome RobH, ER Doc. Paleo eating has nearly cured my chronic RA of 35+years. The meds for it over the years have taken their toll, a big one, and now I have other damage from the meds. Paleo eating has helped tremendously but the CT is starting to kick in and I have never felt better in decades. This is not a joke, it is real. I wish you all the best and APPLAUD you LOUD AND LONG for paying it forward with your patients. it is all we can do, touch those around us and do the best for them.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-16T04:11:43+0200

Hey RobH,

I'm glad you're having success. That is killer!

I've started drawing out the biochemistry of leptin on the back of people's lab sheets. I make a leptin sensitive column: build bone, build muscle, increase sex hormones, better sleep, lower stress, exercise tolerance, decreased appetite, easy weight loss, Hugh Jackman body, etc. Then I show was happens when you have a broken leptin receptor: lousy bone, no muscle, low sex hormones, crappy sleep, high stress, exercise intolerance, high appetite, impossible weight loss, Oprah body, etc. Then everyone asks me, how to become leptin sensitive again. Then we really get somewhere.

### persistence — 2014-03-09T05:53:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114250>

RobH, great to see that there are high quality members here. Having gone through Paleo for the last 2.5 years, it is a great diet, but I would post one caution. Followed strictly, Paleo is really a Trojan Horse ketogenic diet. It's quite \*possible\* to comply with Paleo rules and end up eating only vegetables and no starchy carbs. That ends up being effectively a no carb diet (I will explain below). I followed such a diet and really crashed badly.

I would strongly urge you to go read Paul Jaminet's Perfect Health Diet, which I personally think is one of the most important nutrition books of the last 20 years. It doesn't say anything new, but does a brilliant job of summarizing the literature. One of the PHD key tenets is that the body does best with a certain minimum amount of dietary glucose from starchy carbs. It isn't much, maybe 50 grams per day minimum. They recommend 20% to 30% of diet from starchy carbs, which I find from careful measurement is NOT very much food. I get my starchy carbs from tubers (yams and taro) because of food absorption problems, and 30 grams of carbs from taro is about half of one fist ful of taro.

A key PHD insight is that vegetables that are not starchy carbs have effectively zero carbohydrate. Because the amount of energy it takes to digest them equals their caloric content, they are effectively zero net carb contributors. Eat vegetables for micronutrition, not for macronutrient carb intake.

### caroline — 2014-03-09T07:42:17+0200

### persistence — 2014-03-09T08:39:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114270>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114259)
>
> High quality members??? Ouch...

Caroline, I'm brand new here. So when I open up the very first thread in a forum and see that the poster is an emergency room doctor, yes that is a pretty high quality poster. It tells me people in the medical profession are here reading these forums and Jack's blog. That's not a put down of anyone. That's a thumbs up that the content is such high quality that it attracts people whose life work is medicine.

I've generally noticed that the best nutrition sites are ones that attract both people in health fields (practitioners or researchers) and motivated patients. I'll describe "motivated patients" as people with either above-average interest in nutritional science or serious health issues that traditional medicine doesn't address effectively. The worst sites are the ones that are made of up people who are caught up in diet fads. I'm really excited to have found this resource. I'm reading through some of Jack's articles on the main site and they fit my case perfectly. I think I can learn a lot here.

Welcome!

### RobH — 2014-03-09T19:51:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114343>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=112770)
>
> Thanks Rob for sharing this ....... now we need an update ......please!

Ok, brief update ... more to come

From Feb 20th 2012 or so when I started CT / Paleo (before Epi-Paleo was revealed to the world) / Leptin Rx, without even being "perfect" as my odd job still demands some night hours, fairly high stress, occasional stupid eating mistakes etc, until August 1, 2012. I lost 78 pounds following Jack's protocols. I felt incredible. Like I was 20 years younger. My energy skyrocketed. My friends and family were shocked and amazed (as was I). A whole bunch of them started "following Paleo" and many had varying degrees of success, although none quite like mine, but many weren't as sick as I was to start with (I didn't have any conventional illnesses, only obesity, which we doctors are pretty good about ignoring as an illness or sign of disease). I pointed many to JKs website, and tried to explain that it seemed to be more than diet -- I was rewiring my brain to be more sensitive to Leptin, I was trying to follow circadian biology and light / dark cycles, and most importantly, I was getting cold. Frequently. And enjoying it. I tried in my limited way to explain the benefits I saw from CT and tried to introduce a diabetic friend to it -- he declined -- said he couldn't stand the cold. I tried to get him to start slowly, showed him the CT protocol (dunk your face, etc.) but he never could wrap his mind around it and he continues to rapidly decline from his disease.

Most people took from me that it was the diet. I made a handout and gave it to a bunch of my patients that started to introduce them to the concept of changing diet to reduce inflammation. I pointed them to some of the Paleo sites and Jack's site as the ultimate resource. You must understand this is pretty unusual behavior for an ER doctor (as was wearing funny orange safety glasses during evening and night shifts... I have to wear contacts to do so and generally prefer not to though) so many of my physician colleagues thought I was a little crazy, while at the same time asking me just what i was up to and how I was getting so much better so much faster. But when I told them they chuckled and went back to work.

Found out from some labs (ZRT) that my cortisol levels were very high and so worked hard on reducing them. Started using the Heartmath device, and stopped coffee for a couple of months.

During that time my father passed away. I flew out to visit him on my birthday in April 2012. He had been diagnosed with Stage 4 Colorectal cancer about 5 years before and had availed himself of every bit that modern medicine had to offer. He made it well past his doctor's initial predictions of his survival but that was after an aggressive surgery to resect metastases from his liver, and multiple rounds of truly awful chemotherapy. It was heartbreaking to see my well-educated, rational, wonderful father waste away in front of my eyes. I saw him in March and planned to visit again in May, but got the call in April from his oncologist telling me that he didn't have anything else to offer, and also the call from my mom that she was having a hard time taking care of him. I was supposed to have my first educational consult with Jack that day but emailed him from the airport on the way to take care of my dad, asking him to postpone it a few days.

I had tried during the last month or so of my life to get my dad to change his eating habits and try CT (even though I wasn't even sure it would be good for him but based on Jack's early CT blogs thought it could help, that and some allusions he had made to treating cancer patients with it)

When I got to Arizona to visit my dad I was shocked to see how much he had declined in the last month since I'd seen him. I won't go into extensive detail on the events of the following days but they were pretty tough.

I did have an educational consult with Jack during the next few days. I had asked him on my email to postpone to tell me what I could do to help my dad. I was amazed at he torrent of information he let loose and took 7 pages of notes which I still have. What astounded me was that he had taken my email request very seriously and clearly spent a tremendous amount of time researching some very specific ideas to help with the cancer. And all of them were based on studies and science and reasonable and rational ideas mixed with his unique viewpoint and understanding of medicine, biology, and now I know physics. He came up with a cocktail of supplements that I could try, and suggested some other measures.

This was literally 4 days before my dad passed away and at that point there was no going back and the doctor in me knew that but the son in me wouldn't give up. I took my niece and we went to the local health food store and bought all the supplements. We mixed them up into a smoothie tried very hard to get my dad to take a few sips -- he could barely lift his head but we tried anyway, and I guess trying provided us some solace and helped pass the time during a tremendously difficult week. I know that had I known what I know now (or had I met Jack) 5 years before maybe I could have made a difference in the quality (and quantity) of his life but that is not how the world worked. My dad had the best "conventional" medical care had to offer but it wasn't enough.

I sat by his bedside and administered pain medications until he took his last breath. Fortunately my family was able to fly out to join us and he died a peaceful and dignified death at home, surrounded by loved ones, not in some awful ICU. As a doctor I see death too frequently in the way that nobody should have to undergo it and I am so grateful that my dad wasn't in that setting.

My dad had a PhD in Organic Chemistry and was a professor at a local college. He stayed up late nearly every night working on his lecture notes and grading papers. He sat at his desk next to this ominously humming fluorescent work light which he had had for many years. He stayed up late and then drank hot chocolate right before bed in the winter. He was fascinated by computers and spent many hours in the computer lab with the big mainframes learning to program with punch cards (and introduced me to the same). We were the first people I knew to have a personal computer and he and I spent a lot of time working on it (although it was a green CRT not a nice blue light LED display) together. He always complained that he never slept well.

He came from a long-lived family (his great grandfather died at 102, grandfather at 93, father at 91 or so (see the pattern) so why he got and epithelial cancer at 67 or so is a bit of a mystery... well maybe not so much when you look at some of the information above....

I'll say this again if you are reading this Jack: thank you. You showed a tremendous amount of compassion and you have helped me at that time more than I can possibly express.

I think I will stop now. More to come at some later time.

Thanks for asking.

Best

RobH

### persistence — 2014-03-10T00:14:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114395>

> [RobH said:](/goto/post?id=114344)
>
> I'm not sure I qualify as high quality yet. Working on it though!
>
> RobH

Hi Rob. I lost my father a few years ago as well, and my journey with that was not much different than yours. Now that I have learned so much about nutrition and food intolerance issues, I have come to realize that my father (and grandfather, who also died of intestinal cancer) suffered from very similar problems to the ones I have: wheat intolerance, prediabetes. Both of them in later life developed heart disease, which I suspect was directly related to high insulin / high fasting glucose. I feel bad that I was not able to see their symptoms correctly and intervene when there was still time to affect the outcome.

Having watched two relatives die now, I am familiar with the extremely fragile state of someone in their last month of life. It's heart-breaking to go through this. I understand exactly your sense of desperation to attempt anything possible to extend life. I feel particularly bad for those people whose loved-ones get to a stage where intubation might extend life a few weeks, but usually under horrific conditions for everyone, and without any hope of a different outcome.

I share your opinion about Jack. To me he is exactly what physicians should aspire to be. He is trying his best to help people avoid chronic diseases in the future by paying attention to warning signs in early stage of the disease and taking action while you still can.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T13:11:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114516>

> [Optimalbound said:](/goto/post?id=114514)
>
> Jack has several times called bullshit on Jaminet's musings about "safe starches".
> [http://jackkruse.com/the-rewarding-feeling-of-safe-starches/](http://jackkruse.com/the-rewarding-feeling-of-safe-starches/)

I heard Jimmy Moore say to Professor Cordain once that Paul Jaminet had said that his (Cordain's) diet had given him scurvy and that he'd said that starches this and starches that ...

Professor Cordain basically said, "I don't know of him" (as much as to say, he may move in your world but not in mine) and "My diet can't give you scurvy." A little _de haut en bas_ but fair comment given the provocation I thought. As for starches, Cordain went though the stuff on glycemic load for Mr. Moore.

I don't think it's true to say that "Paleo is really a ... ketogenic diet", Pone. If you look at Cordain's books, and journal papers, he actually recommends quite a lot of carbohydrate. He's not offering just a twist on what, say, Phinney, Volek and Westman are offering. He also reckons that the macronutrient ratios he recommends reflect what current groups of hunter-gatherers actually do. Since Cordain doesn't recommend cereal grains or legumes, principally because of the antinutrients in them, (and doesn't recommend sugar/HFCS either), and fights shy of potatoes because of their glycemic index, he achieves those macronutrient ratios on fruit.

There's nothing, absolutely nothing, in _The Paleo Diet_ or _The Paleo Answer_ on ketosis. The topic isn't even mentioned.

Jack, of course, would not have you eat fruit out of season. Standard Paleo has never talked much about seasonal eating.

### persistence — 2014-03-10T22:18:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114603>

> [Optimalbound said:](/goto/post?id=114514)
>
> Jack has several times called bullshit on Jaminet's musings about "safe starches".
> [http://jackkruse.com/the-rewarding-feeling-of-safe-starches/](http://jackkruse.com/the-rewarding-feeling-of-safe-starches/)

If one diet says "eat no more than 50 grams of starchy carbs" and the other diet says "eat 50 to 120 grams of starchy carbs" these are not radically different diets. They are different degrees of a low-carb diet.

50 grams of carb is a few small slices of yam. It's almost nothing. I think too many people in the Paleo community spend too much time debating differences between diets that are more similar than not.

### persistence — 2014-03-10T22:45:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114605>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=114516)
>
> I heard Jimmy Moore say to Professor Cordain once that Paul Jaminet had said that his (Cordain's) diet had given him scurvy and that he'd said that starches this and starches that ...
>
> Professor Cordain basically said, "I don't know of him" (as much as to say, he may move in your world but not in mine) and "My diet can't give you scurvy." A little _de haut en bas_ but fair comment given the provocation I thought. As for starches, Cordain went though the stuff on glycemic load for Mr. Moore.
>
> I don't think it's true to say that "Paleo is really a ... ketogenic diet", Pone. If you look at Cordain's books, and journal papers, he actually recommends quite a lot of carbohydrate. He's not offering just a twist on what, say, Phinney, Volek and Westman are offering. He also reckons that the macronutrient ratios he recommends reflect what current groups of hunter-gatherers actually do. Since Cordain doesn't recommend cereal grains or legumes, principally because of the antinutrients in them, (and doesn't recommend sugar/HFCS either), and fights shy of potatoes because of their glycemic index, he achieves those macronutrient ratios on fruit.
>
> There's nothing, absolutely nothing, in _The Paleo Diet_ or _The Paleo Answer_ on ketosis. The topic isn't even mentioned.
>
> Jack, of course, would not have you eat fruit out of season. Standard Paleo has never talked much about seasonal eating.

I think Jack's description of Paleo as "bro science" is perfect. The fact is no one can define Paleo exactly. For some it is a high carb diet that uses lots of tubers, rice, fruits. For some it is a low carb diet that stresses under 120 grams of carbs. If you visit forums like Mark's Daily Apple, it is a free for all where Paleo means anything the speaker wants it to mean. When some term can be defined so loosely, it almost stops meaning anything.

It is possible to read some Paleo diet books and end up eating only vegetables that are non-starchy. Because there are no explicit carb targets, the adherent of such a diet does not realize that they are effectively on a ketogenic diet. That's exactly what happened to me. I went on a Paleo compliant diet with zero starchy carbs, lots of nutritious vegetables, animal meats, and lots of healthy fats. It was effectively a ketogenic diet, although no one ever called it that, and I never thought going in that this is what it was.

Whether it was ketogenic or not, I crashed on that diet. I lost a lot of weight that I could not afford to lose, and I have had a hard time getting it back because of other problems I have. I will post a separatel thread on my issues in meet and greet later.

Jaminet isn't perfect either, but his insight that vegetables alone give almost zero net carb utilization over the energy required to digest them really simplifies thinking about carbs. Assuming you have a target of 50 carbs a day, getting those from starchy sources at least guarantees that those 50 grams are above and beyond the amounts needed to digest the starch. And if you are eating 50 to 100 grams of starchy carbs a day, this is a low carb diet. 50 grams of starch a day is nothing. It's four small slices of yam.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T08:35:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114749>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114605)
>
> I think Jack's description of Paleo as "bro science" is perfect.

Bro? Short for broken?

> The fact is no one can define Paleo exactly.

Well, it's just a name; it doesn't really require a "definition". I think Cordain came up with the name as a punchy name for his diet. He was latching on to stuff coming out from Boyd Eaton and collaborators, although Boyd Eaton didn't use that name:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Paleolithic-Prescription-Program-Exercise/dp/0060158719/](http://www.amazon.com/The-Paleolithic-Prescription-Program-Exercise/dp/0060158719/)

I guess Cordain thought: lop the "lithic" off, and it'll make a good short marketing name.

> If you visit forums like Mark's Daily Apple, it is a free for all where Paleo means anything the speaker wants it to mean.

Posters on a forum post what they choose, don't they? Mark Sisson himself doesn't actually use the term. He refers to himself as "primal". _His_ marketing-term, if you like. Mark's version is more like a traditional low-carb diet -- but then most people in the past who ever wrote about prehistoric diets before Boyd Eaton and Cordain came along seem to have considered that before the advent of farming diets were fairly low-carb.

Nothing to stress over anyway. It's just a name. Essentially, to Cordain it seems to mean most of all: I don't advise cereal grains or dairy products, because these are evolutionarily novel foods.

> … I crashed on ... diet \[X\]. I lost a lot of weight that I could not afford to lose, and I have had a hard time getting it back because of other problems I have. I will post a separatel thread on my issues in meet and greet later.

Then why were you asking about going on the Leptin Reset ℞ ?

I may be wrong, but my understanding is that ℞ is for people who are severely _over_\-weight. That's what I understood Jack's blogs on it to say; that's what I understood the book to say; that's what Jack seems to say in podcast interviews.

Mark's version is more like a traditional low-carb diet -- but then most people in the past who ever wrote about prehistoric diets before Boyd Eaton and Cordain came along seem to have considered that before the advent of farming diets were fairly low-carb.

> Then why were you asking about going on the Leptin Reset ℞ ?
>
> I may be wrong, but my understanding is that ℞ is for people who are severely _over_\-weight. That's what I understood Jack's blogs on it to say; that's what I understood the book to say; that's what Jack seems to say in podcast interviews.

I was thinking "Bro" more like two guys talking to each other:

B1: "Hey Bro, I'm on Paleo."
B2: "Cool Bro, I'm on Paleo too."

Brother #1 eats 60% carbohydrates from starchy carbs.
Brother #2 eats a ketogenic diet made of non starchy vegetables.

These diets have very little in common, yet the people in the conversation believe they are the same diet. That's the essence of what Paleo is - to my understanding - in popular forums. People who don't measure or understand macronutrient profiles talk about radically different diets and call them all Paleo because the poster has a caveman in his profile photo. That's what those diets have in common.

Mark Sisson is great. People who post on his forums are confused.

I am skinny not fat. I \*probably\* do not have Leptin resistance. How would I measure that?

I probably \*do\* have insulin resistance, which largely explains why I also have terrible LDL particle counts. Constant high insulin produces too many lipids.

Diabetes affects different people in different ways. The most difficult cases are overweight people because the fat itself becomes implicated in the disease. My case is typical of many over 35: metabolism just gets inefficient as you age. You get insulin resistance. Most people don't understand the implication and that in turn becomes heart disease, cancer, whatever.

I'm trying to correct the condition before it does permanent damage. I'm not more than 20% of the way there yet.

### caroline — 2014-03-11T09:49:19+0200

Dr. jackson says that 98% of people have gut issues and that is where he starts - doesn't even care about testing at first....but hormones play a huge role too - you can't fix one without working on the other

If I may make a suggestion .... you have too many forums and too many paleo experts running amok in your head.
Please read Jack's book and refocus....

Also don't forget - all these so called paleo experts are not clinicians .... Dr. K. works with all of this on a daily basis in his clinic and here on his forum.

### persistence — 2014-03-11T09:57:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114765>

Careful on anorexia. That's a disease of intentional starvation to achieve an aesthetic result. I'm not that for sure. I would love to be 190 pounds. I just can't build muscle, in spite of really trying. My problem is (maybe) some combination of food absorption and intolerance.

What do Saliva ASI and diurnal cortisol tell you? How do I get those?

I assume gastro will do complete gut panel. It will be interesting to see what they order.

Regarding too many forums, I have to point out:

1) I don't have Mark's Daily Apple running in my head. My body and mind ran far away from that nut house a long time ago. So no conflict of interest there.

2) The only other Paleo diet I have mentioned is Perfect Health Diet. People can differ with Jaminet on carbohydrates, but basically he is a lower carb diet and that book does a nice job of summarizing a lot of science. It's not the bible. It's not preventing me from absorbing new ideas or different points of view.

The average blog post written by Jack has about 100 concepts in it. I do understand he is a genius, but even so please allow my posts to deal with at least five concepts without telling me I deal with too many concepts.  I don't think my posts are that tough.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T10:38:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114768>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114760)
>
> I was thinking "Bro" more like two guys talking to each other:
>
> B1: "Hey Bro, I'm on Paleo."
> B2: "Cool Bro, I'm on Paleo too."

So bro is a "cute" nickname -- like bo/beau or whatever?

Well, it's Jack's forum, and he is a neurosurgeon. I'd not say it: I guess it would be abusive as a description of Paleo coming from me. AFAIK, Prof. Cordain spends several hours each working day reading the academic literature as part of his research, and this is material that most people would struggle to follow closely at all. He's spent the past quarter of a century in research in this area. What does that represent? Perhaps 25,000 hours of relevant and highly technical reading -- plus original research done by the teams he leads, like the research they did into acne.

I think Cordain takes the view: I'm a professor at a level one research institute; I'll debate these matters with my equals. That's the impression he gives, whenever when someone like Jimmy Moore organises one of these wild-beast shows and baits him. That was how he came across when Jimmy baited him with what he said Sally Fallon had said. In fact, I listened to what Sally Fallon had said on the previous episode, and Jimmy either forgot the substance of some of these issues or funked out of bringing them up. Perhaps Cordain's manner made him nervous. I don't think either Sally Fallon or Prof. Cordain came well out of it. These shows are publicity, but they're a poisoned chalice. I don't think Jimmy really examines his conscience and asks himself why he does these: they don't really thresh out important issues, and they don't bring harmony and reconcilaition. They probably do help audience figures.

Anyway, baited with: some guy in the paleosphere says your diet gave him scurvy, Cordain could be forgiven for brusqueness.

And can you find me a single qualified medical doctor anywhere who'd state that it could (providing always, that one read the book carefully and implemented what was suggested)?

> ... People who don't measure or understand macronutrient profiles talk about radically different diets ...

Is it necessarily a good idea to be obsessed with measuring and "understanding" macronutrient ratios? I think high-carb diets are inadvisable for most people, and dangerous for some, but as for macronutrient minutiae ... Food quality seems far more important to me.

### sooperb — 2014-03-11T10:47:56+0200

It may start with the belief that being thinner is good but it's much, much more than that. It is a psychiatric disorder that affects the physical self.

### caroline — 2014-03-11T10:49:07+0200

### sooperb — 2014-03-11T11:56:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114779>

I can see that pathway too Mike, it's a disorder of the mind however it originates. As with all things bodily wrong, there is always a cause, the hard part is finding the cause which is where modern medicine goes wrong. It treats the symptoms and often doesn't start looking for a cause until all treatment options have failed. Even then, few medics are able to move across from the physical to the biochemical because they just aren't trained that way. A holistic practitioner has the right approach but probably also hasn't the right knowledge or the tools to investigate.

It has taken someone who has probed deeper and read widely to "join the dots" as he so aptly puts it. There just aren't enough doctors with his awareness, curiousity and determination. I don't know how that can be changed. If you look at the link I posted in the Cave about the decision by a collection of British nutrition experts, who have been assembled it seems by the Government, to disregard the WHO with respect to reducing sugar in the diet, in a cavalier manner with such breathtaking arrogance, how is the message ever going to get across?

My body doesn't support holding glycogen. I cannot sustain enough energy in workouts, with enough intensity or frequency, for the muscle to grow. ### persistence — 2014-03-12T00:12:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114867>

Gagnrad, I have nothing but respect for Cordain. He is nuanced and most of his arguments are clear in scope and structure. I don't have to agree with everything he says to respect him.

The more I study nutrition, the more I realize that 70%+ of good health as you age comes down to glucose, insulin, glucagon, and leptin. Manage these things well and you avoid most of the chronic diseases of aging. You will live as long as your genetics allow, except for a freak statistical outlier event. What makes this hard to do - even if you are motivated - is that we only have a way to measure glucose at home. To get the other three you have to find labs and it doesn't give you much immediate feedback day to day.

At this point - to my thinking - a diet to be worth studying and understanding has to tell a competent story about how it manages those four things. Love them or hate them, Dr Rosedale, Paul Jaminet, and Jack Kruse all have diets that closely tie to these concepts and tell a competent story about how to control these elements. You can differ with them on many details, but in the big picture they are low carbohydrate diets. They are serious people with serious ideas, and we all learn from them. Macronutrient ratios are a critical theme in all of these diets. For Rosedale, he wants you to be ketogenic. For Kruse, apparently he wants you no more than 50 gm of starchy carbs per day, preferably less. For Jaminet, 50 grams to 120 grams of carbs, with a typical target being about 20% of total caloric intake in carbs. If you want to hedge bets, you could be at 50 gm of carbs and probably all three of them would say you are at least close to compliance.

I respect Mark Sisson a lot because he is also nuanced and is not dogmatic. But the people on his forums are largely just trend followers who can't define their diet with any consistency. Some of the people on his forums are eating extremely high carbohydrate diets. They have no frigging clue whether they are prediabetic, and if they were they wouldn't even care. I have had people on those forums tell me "Why do you worry so much about blood sugar? You need to just get outside and smell fresh air." So Paleo diet for such a person means trying to enjoy life, and eat natural foods, but high in carbohydrates, and if there are a lot of fruits, that is also high in fructose and sugars. People in such forums also sometimes make recipes with agave sugar because it is "natural", so let's add some pure liquid sucrose into the fire.

I apologize for being difficult. But I find it difficult to talk to such people. They are killing themselves with uncontrolled carbohydrates and don't want to invest a single neuron in understanding anything. I am very flawed. I have very limited understanding of things. At least I am trying to solve the right problems, or am open to understanding what the right problems are so that I can solve them.

### persistence — 2014-03-12T00:20:27+0200

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114770)
>
> What you said about anorexia is monumentally unfair and stupid - to be blunt ...you have no idea what you are talking about.

Let's try to correct my mistakes and I apologize.

From Wikipedia, the first line definition of anorexia nervosa is:

"**Anorexia nervosa** is an [eating disorder](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eating_disorder) characterized by immoderate [food restriction](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calorie_restriction), inappropriate eating habits or rituals, obsession with having a thin figure and an irrational fear of[weight gain](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weight_gain), as well as a distorted body self-perception."

I do not restrict food. I eat a lot of calories and would be happy to eat more if I could.

I do not have an obsession with being thin. In fact the opposite, I would like to be about 190 pounds of lean muscular mass.

I do not have an irrational fear of weight gain. I want to gain weight not lose it.

If you think Wikipedia has a wrong definition of anorexia, I am not the person to argue that. Their definition seems to have a lot in common with every other publication I have seen. I know almost nothing about anorexia, I'm sorry.

By any reasonable definition, I am not anorexic.

### sooperb — 2014-03-12T11:30:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114974>

Anorexia is a state of mind not of body. The mind controls the body, in the case of anorexia it restricts eating to a dangerous level, it is however not about eating per se, it's about control. Wikipedia is a useful tool but remember that it is all from unverified contributors. I could write a whole page about anorexia but it wouldn't mean that what I wrote is correct or factual.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T11:37:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114975>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=114974)
>
> Anorexia is a state of mind not of body. The mind controls the body, in the case of anorexia it restricts eating to a dangerous level, it is however not about eating per se, it's about control. Wikipedia is a useful tool but remember that it is all from unverified contributors. I could write a whole page about anorexia but it wouldn't mean that what I wrote is correct or factual.

First assumption made there: that mind and body are two things and not one. That's questionable right off the bat.

[http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/dualism/](http://plato.stanford.edu/entries/dualism/)

### sooperb — 2014-03-12T11:49:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114981>

Like your hand and your arm? Your foot and your leg? Connected but separate?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T13:13:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-er-doc-from-california.173/post-114987>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=114981)
>
> Like your hand and your arm? Your foot and your leg? Connected but separate?

It might be closer than that. It might be simply that mind and body are the _same_ thing seen from two different aspects.

There are all kinds of experiences that suggest that. There are also some arguments that do.

Otherwise, there's a dualist view: that the mind is in the body like a sailor in a ship. That's Descartes' formulation, but the notion is much older, and comes into Western thought through Plato.

But you end up with problems, such as: if the mind is something fundamentally different from the body how does the one act upon the other? People tried to fudge that by positing some intermediate substance, failing to see that that doesn't solve the problem. How does the intermediate substance act on both ends of the bridge it forms? Back where we started.

Medieval Christian beliefs are sometimes expressed in terms of a body/soul dualism, but it's really coming out of Plato. AFAIK, the Jews didn't really think in that way.

After Descartes the dualist view was fairly firmly imbedded in philosophy, but began to be challenged in different ways later on. William James does it one way, Wittgenstein in another.

But I'm not a philosopher, and I don't take a view on it. I'd sooner say I don't know. I don't think there's any firm grounds to believe in dualism, though.

It's a different issue, but I am sceptical of "the talking cure" in just about anything. I think it may help as part a wider approach. Its results don't seem to be impressive. Even assuming that, say, a Freudian understanding of a particular problem someone might have was correct (dubious, IMO), does understanding necessarily bring resolution? I think psychotherapists have made some very large claims but have let a lot of people down while making pretty large inroads into their wallets.

I have heard that anorexia is another condition for which there have been reports that neurofeedback is helpful. If that can be substabtiated, I think that would be interesting. Apparently, the University of Graz is currently looking into it:

[http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01390493](http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01390493)

BE CAREFUL!!!! don't be afraid to go slow. I've had to quit ALL CT because of what I've only realized now seems to be a VERY nasty head full of cold urticaria from either going too quick or too cold. I'd gotten as far a a few tubbings without a problem until i dropped the tub temp just a little too far or too long and whump: over 2 weeks of spreading, painful, swollen, crazy itchy rash behind ears, back of head and down back of neck. My naturopath suggested neem oil yesterday and that's FINALLY beginning to give a bit of relief. Was it just the CT? the combination of CT and BHRT (which I've also stopped)? god only knows, but I'm wiser and a little fearful! I'd love to see some cautions and suggestions from Dr. Kruse in addition to the group's "gung ho, we're in this together"
