### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-05-26T01:05:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-27488>

On 5-24 was my 11th trip up the mtn. It was quite windy and we were told to stay close by. I had fun climbing around on the rocks and adjusting my ankles and knees to my hiking boots. 02 levels were about 88-96. No dizziness and only one bout of nausea noted. Later I had some chips and cheese dip. Afterward walking was a little difficult and no dizziness or blackout potential noted.

One 5-25 was my 12th trip up the Mtn and 3rd day in a row. This time it was a tad more windy and we swayed into the restraining bar about 4 inches from the extreme mark. Yikes. As soon as we got there they announced no one else would go up and they began to take people down. I watched the first group go down and thought if the gondola did a loop de loop I was gonna walk down! Actually it was very safe. They put a lot of extra water ballast and the ride was very safe. I amused my self while waiting for the last ride by checking out how my new sock combo and how tighter lacing of the shoes in a certain spot would hold my feet better. I was so interested in checking out the boots I didn't realize how much I was walking and doing just fine. No nausea, gasping, or potential blackout issues. I paused once on the last set of stairs and that was all. In addition I did several loads of clothes and went to my PT appointment before I left. What a difference in my energy from 2 months ago. Last year when I finished my 3 month job I was practically on bed rest for a month. This year I kept right on going. Interesting note is that I am not doing anything more up on the mtn than slowly strolling around for an hour and then sitting and looking at the scenery and chatting with the tourists for a half hour or so. This is my kind of exercise!

### vkiernan — 2012-05-26T05:22:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-27573>

> **TerryF;27573 wrote:** Hi Vkiernan, thanks for the interest. What is your optimal?

That's a loaded question for sure. My optimal is to get rid of my adrenal fatigue, fix my gut, balance my hormones and have my thyroid back on track. A lot to work on for sure. I believe I have a fairly long road ahead of me but on a positive note at least I know most of what needs fixing. You give me hope for sure. You just keep on a chuggin'. I want to be able to play with my grandaughters and not be overwhemed with exhaustion and stress. They are 5 and 7 and just like my own. I love those babes so much. It is hard right not cuz they wear me out and then I am done for the day and have nothing left. I also want to not be cranky but all that ails me makes me that way. I have made a lot progress but there is more to be gained. My optimal really is to wake up happy and want to smile. That will be a grea day for sure. When that happens, I will know everything else is making progress and I will be able to do the things I want to do and enjoy it.

I am CT'ing as much as I can to help me with all of my issues and it is working for sure on some. I have been doing the LRx since July of last year. I didn't know then everything I know now about how bad things really are so the reset for me has been slow. I am still plugging along though and will continue. I know this path is the best one out there and I totally trust Dr. K.

I live in the midwest now so I won't be climbing any mountains anytime soon but I have been to Glacier Nat. Park many times and having you talk about climbing the mountain brings back great memories. I love it on top of the mountain; not so much at the bottom though. Thinking back, I remember the beautiful streams and lakes, the wild flowers that flow endlessly in the breeze and the occasionally mound of snow in August that begs to be sat in. My mom and my brother came to visit me and we all went up there in the month of September way back when and they were standing in snow up to their thighs. It was a great experience for all since they were from the midwest too.

Sounds like you have done a fair amount of travel yes??? Sorry I took so much of your space. You just got me thinkin' is all.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-05-26T07:26:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-27599>

Enjoyed your comments. While fixing adrenal fatigue etc. is certainly admirable what makes it all worthwhile is the thought of playing with the grandbabies and seeing them graduate from college, the visits to the mountains, and the visuals of the wildflowers which by the way are great where I am. They are in tiny little mounds of yellow, white and lavender sprinkled all over the mtn top. Here's to being optimal!

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-05-28T20:39:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-28069>

Had to miss 5-26 as wind was very high. Went up 5-27 Sunday for 13th visit up to 10,378 feet. Lots of tourists as it's memorial weekend. I'm focusing on strengthening my feet, ankles and knees so i went up and down on the stones. Saw lots of wild flowers. (Note to self, get a flower ID book). No noticible dizziness, or nausea. I don't want to concentrate on this now. Before I knew it I had been moving about for an hour and 20 minutes. That I could feel. Someone even asked if I was feeling alright. I sat down inside for the last half hour. In addition, I wore only a tee shirt and carried a white sunproof shirt. Again, I was concerned as I reached the top about the cold. The wind was at least 25 mph, temp at top was about 34 deg F. Windchill was about 25 deg. I could feel the bite of the cold and my hands immediately became icy. But I brought my hiking gloves too. I put them on and was very comfortable in my tee shirt, hands became warm, amazing. I think that's how the Sherpa kids became cold acclimatized. It doesn't seem to take long either. Total time at top this time 3 hours, some due to heavy traffic to get on the tram to come down. Didn't check 02 level as mostly felt OK. I took off my sunglasses most of this time as I went in and out of the shade and sun against greenery. I'm only wearing sunglasses during driving for the glare otherwise they're off. If I wear the blu-blockers too much I get agitated.

### caroline — 2012-06-17T09:56:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-31723>

I am enjoying reading your story so much - thankyou. I just came back from a month in Tibet and I will never be the same \[good thing\] The people are so beautiful. It was such a joyful and spiritual trip and I loved every single second. However - it was impossible to eat paleo and I am struggling to get back to where I was. Not eating paleo brought back all my aches and pains and arthritis. It was quite stunning to see how fast it returned.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-07-24T00:03:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-40490>

> **caroline;31829 wrote:** I am enjoying reading your story so much - thankyou. I just came back from a month in Tibet and I will never be the same \[good thing\] The people are so beautiful. It was such a joyful and spiritual trip and I loved every single second. However - it was impossible to eat paleo and I am struggling to get back to where I was. Not eating paleo brought back all my aches and pains and arthritis. It was quite stunning to see how fast it returned.

Hi Caroline, thanks for the interest. i just got back from vacation at sea level, I felt like Tarzan!. As I was climbing around on top of the mtn earlier I thought that this must be what the Sherpa kids do and they get naturally acclimated. they just play around and reap the benefits. Given you were eating their diet I wonder if the altitude they were acclimated to played a part in preventing any aches and pains on their part. How high did you go up in Tibet? Of course they don't eat any processed food I bet. Did you just eat their food? I know when i traveled in China and India I took all kinds of bars of packaged food and that sort of thing played havoc with my aches and pains. This time since I stayed in the US I took some supplemental whey and collagen protein powder. I barely used half of what I took. Since I'm fat adapted I could stop eating whenever I wanted to so could avoid eating the wrong thing. i mostly ate white fish and buffalo steaks. Eating twice a day was so helpful. If I did more activity I added a tablespoon of MCT or coconut oil in mid afternoon.

Talk about aches and pains! The first three days I thought I would die. I slept in a pup tent and crawling in and out was excruciating! Since I came by plane I had about an inch of padding off the ground. I could have bought another pad but I decided to tough it out. Amazingly by the 4th day it got a lot easier and my sore shoulder even felt better. At the end of the 10 days after all the rain, thunder and lightening (and me under the tree) I felt quite well and no aches and pains.

My mattress is due to be replaced and I'm giving it second thoughts due to cost. I had some really supportive pillows out there on the ground and even my neck pain got better. Hmmm.

Hi TerryF -I am loving hearing about what you are doing! You are a very motivated woman! When in Tibet we went to Mt Everest base camp - so -5300M approx. Everyone seems so healthy there - the children are gorgeous. We ate alot of rice and noodles. I think there is a lot of yack meat and fat there - but they didn't seem to be giving that to us. They also drink a ton of yak butter tea.

so they are getting lots of fat in there diet and no junk. I am going to Uzbekistan next. I think there will be more variety of food - but it will still be a problem for me. I do take coconut oil with me and contemplated taking cans of salmon - but I think it is impossible to take enough stuff for 26 days. suitcase Weight is a factor as well.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-07-29T21:10:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-41836>

@Caroline. thanks for the info. so that's about 15,000 feet? If I can suggest. eat as much yak butter as you can. They have salmon and tuna in soft pacs that may weigh less so you can get your protein in. If you are not fat adapted yet, make every effort to do so as you will not have as much hunger. You could ask about their meat and fat. They may think others won't eat that stuff so they don't serve it. Have fun, I'm jealous.

### caroline — 2012-07-30T11:03:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-41915>

Thanks TerryF - I did drink the yack butter tea a few times - but it is very salty and smelly. definitely something to work on! I will try to become fat adapted before I go. Uzbekistan will be quite a bit different with much more variety and fresh fruit and veg. I am going to have coffee beforehand with the woman who is leading the tour and so I can get a handle on what will be available and plan accordingly. looking forward to hearing more of your escapades! Cheers from down under

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-08-01T04:55:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-42203>

Well, I'm back home and recovering from mold exposure in Michigan and Cleveland OH. I'm not sure but I think the hallucinations came from this. I haven't checked yet. I've had periods of sneezing, coughing etc. since I've been back for last two weeks. I've also been feeling feverish but no temperature increase. I finally got the HSCRP done and it is 2.45. Yikes. I'm eating a low inflammatory diet and trying to get to sleep at a decent time. Oh yes, My BG is way up. used to be 89 FBS last few days it's been 135, 121 etc. My BP has been up too. Maybe I will have to rethink going to live in Cleveland to be near family. I have started ice packs last two nights. Today I went back up the mtn and stayed 45 minutes, I believe this is about the 14th visit. First in about a month. I sat and read a book about 45 minutes. I then felt nauseated as I walked back to the tram. heart rate was about 110 upon walking. Guess I have to readjust. I'm still having episodes of vaso vagal syncope (I think that's what it is called.) I'm getting the 23and me test then will make that appt with Dr K. I am having a hard time adjusting to the altitude. Much more difficult than I thought it was going to be. I've lived here in the high desert for 15 years too! Dr K will probably say it's hormones as I am in that menopausal age group. Sigh.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-08-04T18:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-42858>

Aah well, sad to say diabetic symptoms are still here and alive and well. I'm still at about 50 gm CHO per day. Todays FBS is 140. The trip was very stressful more so than I thought. Dr K told me to re-read CT-13 and CT-11. As I traveled around doing my errands the next day I realized my situation was similar to Kevin's although I wasn't in any sort of training situation. I unknowingly self-selected for inactivity because any time I tried a new exercise or activity I had over training symptoms such as sore throat, cough, stuffy nose, feeling of unwellness etc. This time on the trip I had no choice but to keep up activity daily. This day as I traveled about the city I realized this has been going on for at least 15 years. I recall being slightly annoyed that any physical activity did this but never thought to connect it to inflammation. I look at least 15 years younger than I am but inside I always feel like I am on the edge of getting sick.

The HSCRP was a real eye opener for me. My labs average is 1-3 and mine is 2.45 which isn't above average but it is far from what Dr K recommends below 0.03. Another thing that is helping is the excell spreadsheet I've done with my labs and the medical history I am putting together for the appointment.

I have part of the protocol in place so I'm not starting from scratch. After dark, check. Diet, check. Labs, almost check. Appt with Dr K, next check. CT, ummm, not yet but I have made several ice/alcohol packs. I re-read CT protocol and every ones suggestions, I started torso icing on 8-2 once a day. Today I want to make two more of the big ones for torso icing. Dr K said to keep ice on self as much as possible. I'm keeping track of my tolerance too as I have read many comments on this. I'm doing face dunks twice a day for the next two weeks and figure out what it takes to get water temp to 45 F so my skin temp is appropriate. Next I have to decide on how best to decrease the inside home temp and to remind myself to keep car temp low and maybe to get an ice vest. To prepare for the in water sessions I have to decide what I can use as a compression shirt being female. I have one that just compresses the torso and is meant to be worn with a bra. This may work. I want to retest HSCRP in a month because I want to focus on this even though they recommend 3 months.. In addition, I start another 6 week contract job then.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-08-04T19:07:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-high-desert-sw-new-mexico-usa-hcg-labs.248/post-42862>

Oh yes, magnesium. I've started to soak in it, and added to the am in addition to the regular pm dose.

# Thread: How to apply Crossfit properly
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/>

**Posts:** 33

### Bernadette — 2012-03-18T18:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-2398>

This morning Dr Kruse posted this comment on Facebook

> Cross fit is ok if applied properly. In my view it rarely is. So it can be dangerous.

I am well aware of the pitfalls of Crossfit and how people can push themselves too far with it... I was interested in any expanded thoughts Dr K might have on the subject, such as what it looks like properly applied

### Glamorama — 2012-03-19T05:22:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-2610>

I have no idea I would imagine it means no more than twice weekly and only in short bursts?

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-19T19:00:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-2809>

Excellent question. And I would love the answer as well.

That said, I think CF is changing culturally now that it has the Reebok endorsement and The Games have become a focus. Crossfit is becoming - IMHO - less about functional fitness for every body and more about PR's, performance, competition. The number of people who compete in The Open is high. Yet there is a WOD with Muscle UPs? Seriously. The focus in Crossfit has moved to being the "sport of fitness" and that means competition. That is mentally light years away from joining a group of people to work out together and get into the best shape of your life.

Every box is programmed differently - as it should be - because it should be tailored to the box members. But even within a box there is going to be a variety of athlete levels and it takes a great coach to be able to program for the 40 year old newbie who needs to lose 40 lbs and the 27 year old who does butterfly pullups.

IMHO I think the principles of strength training with weights 2-3 times a week and some sort of metcon/sprinting 1-2 times a week may well be within Dr. K's guidelines. So IF, and it is a big IF you can get than done within the programming of a CF box, great. Otherwise, it is time to pony up and find a personal trainer if you need help with proper form and then start programming your own exercise.

Just my two cents. And I do hope Dr K weighs in on this.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-19T19:34:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-2831>

> **cgk;2818 wrote:** Excellent question. And I would love the answer as well.
>
> That said, I think CF is changing culturally now that it has the Reebok endorsement and The Games have become a focus. Crossfit is becoming - IMHO - less about functional fitness for every body and more about PR's, performance, competition. The number of people who compete in The Open is high. Yet there is a WOD with Muscle UPs? Seriously. The focus in Crossfit has moved to being the "sport of fitness" and that means competition. That is mentally light years away from joining a group of people to work out together and get into the best shape of your life.
>
>
>
> Every box is programmed differently - as it should be - because it should be tailored to the box members. But even within a box there is going to be a variety of athlete levels and it takes a great coach to be able to program for the 40 year old newbie who needs to lose 40 lbs and the 27 year old who does butterfly pullups.
>
>

> IMHO I think the principles of strength training with weights 2-3 times a week and some sort of metcon/sprinting 1-2 times a week may well be within Dr. K's guidelines. So IF, and it is a big IF you can get than done within the programming of a CF box, great. Otherwise, it is time to pony up and find a personal trainer if you need help with proper form and then start programming your own exercise.
>
>
>
> Just my two cents. And I do hope Dr K weighs in on this.

These are all excellent observations, and I feel I am in the minority with my CF gym. We are the only one I know that is NOT encouraging the entire place to participate in the Open. I want my members safe and injury-free, thank you! I do have individuals who are competition worthy, and they can workout with a rank beginner, but they are doing different workouts. I know (even if they don't) that they have different goals, abilities, injury/medical histories etc. I get pissed sometimes when CF gets this bum rap, 'cause I think, "hey! MY gym isn't like that!" but I know I am a little fish in the big sea. I program 6 weeks at a time. I use periodization in my cycles.

Bernadette, I would find out about local CF gyms, ask if they do their own programming. If they do, good. Ask if they have strength only classes. If they do, good. If they don't, ask if you can only do the strength portions of their workouts. So if it is back squat 3-3-3 followed by a metcon, can you squat and then leave? Would they be willing to write a program for you? Strength plus limited conditioning.

If you are doing CF from home, you should have bumpers, squat rack etc and do a strength program 2-3 x wk depending, with limited conditioning, less than 12 minutes 1-2 x wk

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-21T00:46:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-3593>

My gym is the mountain. There's less MRSA lurking there. Plus I can throw rocks and logs and no one stares.

Most people have this idea in their mind (I know because I used to torture myself through 5-6 triathlons a year) that you have to kill yourself in the gym or run marathons to get rid of your belly, get ripped muscles, etc. However, this exercise paradigm is not true. Body composition is mostly a diet and sleep affair, not gym hours. Mark Sisson says it 80 percent diet and 20 percent exercise. He's probably right. (And who wouldn't want to look like Mark Sisson?) Wink.

If Kruse's CT thing plays out, then exercise plays even less of a role.

Paleo and Cross-fit are kinda linked in a lot of people's minds. This is unfortunate. My sister asked me if she went Paleo, does this mean she has to "go" Cross-fit, too. Umm...hell no.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-22T14:59:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-4332>

> **Bernadette;3733 wrote:** Also, though the attitude of our trainers isn't simply 'make 'em feel like they got the crap kicked out of them', I definitely think there is an underlying expectation that people should be able to do 5 days a week, which I know my body just can't handle.

In my gym, which is small granted, most memebers do 2-3 x week. We do have a few that come 5 days or so, and we (the coaches) take care to ratchet their intenstiy down on certain workouts, so they are not going \*all out\* on every workout. Every few cycles will have them take their intensity down 50% for the entire week. They are usually unwilling to do so at first, then in retrospect, realize how beneficial that time was.

I dislike the "make 'em feel like they got the crap kicked out of them" attitude you mention, and boy, is it prevalent. Some workouts I see just make me cringe. And the YELLING, even berating, from the "coaches". ugh. My philosophy is to make every person who enters my gym feel welcome upon entering and feel about themselves when they leave.

Not like we're a bunch of lotus eating, praise-everybody-who-does-anything, everyone-gets-a-finisher's-medal kind of place LOL but I see no need to harrass and goad clients into doing more than they can/want/need to do. Basically I took what I percieved to be the big problems from the CF gyms I had attended and did the OPPOSITE of that. I was made to feel inferior for wanting to scale, ignored at times because I wasn't a competitor, and yelled at, in a way that I was supposed to see as "motivational", but had the opposite effect on me.

I wouldn't give up on CF, I'd keep looking Bernadette. Or start building a home gym

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-03-23T14:55:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-4921>

cgk, and that would be about the ONLY reason to move here! This is the most corrupt, expensive, crowded state EVER! Ugh.

.... but I do love the shore ....

And while I agree with Jack about the education, it isn't about a piece of paper from a program or institution. The head coach at the gym has no formal degree ( I have my BS in exercise science) yet has extensively "schooled" himself in physics, chemistry, biology, metabolism, nutrition. And like many other coaches, we have lots of "certifications" but so do other coaches. I guess it comes down to what the coaches goals are, personally and for their clients. I do not expect to "breed" a Games competitor, and I have zero desire to do CF competitions. I don't think that is a healthy option, as a long term goal. (short term, maybe) I'm not looking for notoriety as a "bad-ass coach" who makes up these ridiculously difficult workouts. If I'm honest, I guess I want people to say "wow, that programming is solid" or "sheesh, her people are strong" And as far as my clients, I want them to remain injury free, lead healthy low stress lives, and have a balanced perspective on their workouts: as in, not every workout is gonna be a homerun, nor does it need to be. PRs are fun, but aren't the be all/end all of training. (hmmm, maybe I need to remind myself of that LOL)

Anyway, cgk, if you did meander up to NJ for any reason; ie. passing through on a road trip 'cause why would anyone make it a destination (hee hee), certainly drop in. Love to have you.

### Leevy13 — 2015-06-28T22:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-169788>

I'm glad I found this thread. CrossFit changed my life and I have a real passion to continue doing it. However, I want to be smart about it. The owner at my box put it best. He said (and I'm paraphrasing), You don't have to come in here and kill yourself every day. You may go hard for two days in a row, then come in on the third day and only workout with the PVC pipe. You want to work on proper technique and mobility. We don't want to run people off by burning them out or injuring them. That's not the spirit of our box.
I'm far more focused on the functional movement aspect. I want to be as fit as possible, but not at the expense of my joints and definitely not at the expense of my nervous system. I noticed that this is an old thread, so if you guys have any updates or current advice, I would love to hear from you. Thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-29T04:09:10+0300

### JoeBranca — 2015-07-20T20:59:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-172444>

Hi Leevy, not so sure I have 'expertise' but I'll throw in some comments.

I can't speak for any one particular crossfit box, but my sense is in general CF's main goal is exercise-as-competitive-sport. This is not an optimal approach for a general population trying to get themselves out of a hole. Combine that with little emphasis on being connected to nature, and I don't consider that fitness in an evolutionary direction.

Crossfit also promotes itself as preparation for the real world. but in reality IMHO the functional exercises executed for time or volume mainly prepares one for real world strength and conditioning contests, or real world boot camps (military, law enforcement). Of course there will be _carry over_ from CF strength and conditioning and metcons to real world situations (provided you don't get too injured in the process), but a properly executed "functional" movement is not the same thing as a practical movement, which I define as an innately stable movement pattern that optimally and efficiently couples our body plans with real environments, adapatable to whatever imposed demands may come, not just boxes. Many of the kinds of exercises Crossfit workouts consist of for time or volume are best appropriate for met-con addicts who do not plan to get a lot of mileage out of their bodies (examples: olympic lifts for time, barbell deadlifts performed in a pre-exhausted state?).

The real problem I think - philosophically - is approaching physical movement only in context of competition. This is of course not just CF but common in the modern fitness and athletics worlds... interestingly its very rare to non-existent among observed hunter gatherer societies. Technique and movement skill would lend itself either to play (challenging, but not a competitive structure selecting for individual winners) or practical utility, which is dictated by the "conditions of existence".

I'd imagine the rare crossfit gigs "doing it right" would be the ones that are way more serious about movement quality -- that is, focus on technique so that it is safe, energy efficient (focus on making movement as easy as possible for the given task, not feeling destroyed as a performance goal), _and_ performance is good. A true multi-functional approach to fitness, meaning the exercises are making one more _adaptive not just more conditioned._ Perhaps if a CF is bringing in things like yoga as well that would be a good sign they really want to explore movement. And the community spirit isn't built around folks commiserating about smashing their heads against the wall, so to speak, 3-4x / week.

### thomas — 2015-07-21T19:43:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-172536>

I read that some movements in crossfit carry with them a high risk of injury. aka not the way we're designed to move.

Obviously risk of injury depends mostly on the force you apply. which is dependant on mass and acceleration. So if you move really quickly or with a high weight or both it's likely you're gonna injure yourself. Lifting 10kg in half a second is more dangerous than lifting 20kg in 5 seconds.

### JoeBranca — 2015-07-25T00:08:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-172909>

A training mindset is huge and not discussed often enough... training can be a way to self benefit and look down on your fellow man, or it can be a way to self-give and lift up your fellow man. This was posted in a Movnat forum recently:

> I finished level one certification yesterday with Kellen Milad and Erwan in Santa Monica, CA. While walking to get lunch today, I found an elderly homeless man passed out on the sidewalk in the blazing sun, clearly burning deeper his already burned skin. Frankly he was covered in urine and filth, but with Erwan Le Corre's message of love resonating deeply inside my soul, he felt like my brother, my father, my uncle, and my son. I was able to wake and communicate with him just enough to get his permission to allow me to lift and carry him to the safety of shade. Deadlift to Waist Carry became something real, something tangible, something profoundly human for me. Once I set him down and arranged his belongings, he thanked me and honestly I cried in gratitude for this wondrous human body I inhabit.
>
> All of my pushing to be strong as a way to "perfect" myself from the world fell away to become a path towards humanity and kinship with creation. Thank you from the bottom of my heart to the Mov Nat community, to Erwan and to Kellen for inspiring me to be strong to be wholehearted. No one is free until we are all free.

Quite different from this:

[http://therxreview.com/the-uglier-side-of-crossfit/](http://therxreview.com/the-uglier-side-of-crossfit/)

### René Borg — 2015-08-20T17:33:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-175209>

For those who enjoy MovNat (Erwan's work) also have a look at Ido Portal's work. I attended his weekend workshop after doing my MovNat Trainer education and while it is quite different it is a fantastic system for learning 'Movement' as a language. He calls it 'Movement X' (Movement Culture) and he is a remarkable teacher. What his students achieve in terms of moving with grace and power is impressive.

Ido has a good axiom: 'the arms manipulate, the legs locomote, the spine organises' and this informs a lot of his approach on how to get the best out of each subsystem. Its a heuristic of course, so imperfect, but as a rule of thumb it has served me really well with clients especially in understanding what type of skill training and conditioning is most effective in movements where one of these three areas dominate (i.e. crawling patterns and climbing/pulling is generally best for the upper body/hands, sprinting/running for legs and so on).

If somehow MovNat and Movement X could mate and have a love child we'd be close to a perfect system (knowing we'll never get that...)

### Danco3636 — 2015-08-22T02:58:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-175353>

> [My anatomy PSA for today: the arms guide, the legs move but the spine organizes all human movement. What organizes the spine? Light water and magnetism that dictates how the neocortex is organized in sensory and motor cortices. Carry on.
>
> Dr. Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse)Many believe that exercise and caffeine can shrink vessels in the subarachnoid space of the brain bathed in CSF.......but what happens to the neocortex is the key to understanding how movement can occur or cannot occur......When the redox potential of the person is optimized or good the cerebrovascular bed also shrinks.......but in most people it gets larger because their redox potential is suboptimal in our modern world (we can see this in functional MRI now)......This action steals electrons from CSF to provide them to the vessels and neural pathways controlling sensory and motor cortices when we exercise.........This is why many with poor redox potentials die, get injured easier, and move improperly when we watch them. This is an epidemic in modern young pro sports. Consider all the injuries that just happened this week in NFL camps. Today we see more young professional athletes get injured because they are all addicted to blue lit video games, carbohydrates 24/7, and excessive training. this is why cross fit now markets its own line or orthotics. It is becoming obvious too all those who are not brain dead and believe modern paradigms.
> When the neocortex struggles for its "electron fix" the result is a headache or poor neural coordination of the motor and sensory systems to generate natural movement. Cross fit build zoo movements. This is why Ido Portal and MovNat people can do things that cross fitters can not do. It has very little to do with your level of fitness and has a lot to do with the movements being congruent with your CNS and PNS design. This is why how Ido and Erwan move is rarely seen in the modern world. Some people can increase electron flux via coffee or exercise in an indoor lit crossfire box.....hence why they are drawn to use it, but this does not mean it is optimal or ideal. The underlying biophysics tells us the environment they exist in and do these things are the real problem..........they ignore and injuries result. Pills and surgery can't fix environmental deficits ever.

**BOOM!**

### René Borg — 2015-08-26T13:15:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-175775>

Great summary Jack. A question related to my question on the Achilles thread: if light, water and magnetism are the main drivers of proper motor control because they setup the 'winning terrain' for the spine, where do you see motor skill relearning.

I suppose I am asking:

1\. Would you: Setup the 'winning terrain' (body in tune with the three natural laws) and then start rehabilitating the patient's motor skill through techniques (such as Ido Portal's micro exploration of the spinal movements etc.)
2\. Or would you: just Setup the winning terrain and then simply encourage natural lifestyle in the confidence the body will reorganise its own movement in the ideal way now that the 'pipe is clean'

### Dan E — 2016-04-27T02:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-apply-crossfit-properly.266/post-193633>

I think I can add some valuable thoughts to this thread. I had the fortunate experience of living near one of the best Crossfit boxes on Long Island and began training there in 2011. I gained some valuable knowledge that lead me from one amazing teacher to the next. I will list them so you can check them out.

It was love at first sight as I walked through the door. It was a gym with almost nothing in it. A big room of pull-up bars, gymnastic rings, climbing ropes and squat racks; the least amount of equipment of any gym I had ever seen. I had studied weight lifting for almost 20 years, and this seemed to begin my education. I learned about the proper mechanics of running, jumping, squating, rowing, gymnastics, mobility, recovery, you name it, and the coaching was excellent. Within 6 months my lungs and heart were in the best shape of my life and my physique was catching up also.

These discoveries also had a dark side. The more I trained, the more I needed to. I think this is the slippery slope of dopamine deficiency. After a while, I only felt alright when I had the rush of endorphins after a workout, and so I never wanted to miss a day. We were advised to train three times per week max but I'd often double that. My coffee consumption rose to an all time high. I did incur many injuries as the sport is often criticized for, I think it was more age related. The ideas discussed here on this site would be a godsend to the community because they are truly looking for optimal.

There are many positive aspects and there are many unintended consequences. If you can find the right coaching, there is a huge advantage as you can learn a ton without having to pay a personal trainer. These coaches can and should give you homework including books and video tutorials.

Kelly Starrett mobility wod
[http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLB67wXqPqtMcNVA-UndNUy7bahGJzLQbT](http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLB67wXqPqtMcNVA-UndNUy7bahGJzLQbT)

Jagroops mobility class

Brian MacKenzie
[http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLKWVooneqr-uPZleSPxUHFIpB0eljK2a\_](http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLKWVooneqr-uPZleSPxUHFIpB0eljK2a_)

Mark Rippetoe
[http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNhFKPjedRnQ0fZAf1v\_K6uCPICapzzJc](http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNhFKPjedRnQ0fZAf1v_K6uCPICapzzJc)

Dr Romanov pose method running
[https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLGE4JfVVA0XzotFjh3hKw1Osb33aELoy6](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLGE4JfVVA0XzotFjh3hKw1Osb33aELoy6)

I can't tell you how much I learned from their books and videos. Crossfit might go off the rails when it involves intense competition but there's no pressure to get involved. Like in any sport, recovery is paramount, and if you don't know, you learn the hard way. I also made many friends and acquaintances with a positive and healthy outlook. As an adult where can you find people like that?

I have left the gym and since I have learned so much about light, I have taken my [www.ironmaster.com](http://www.ironmaster.com)
dumbbells and kettlebell to the woods, beach or high school track to do interval training based on what I learned at Crossfit. It's not as fun as training with a bunch of people but until I drag someone out there with me, it will be just me and the sun throwing weight around. I really like the movenat idea and will be searching that out in the future also. If lifting doesn't appeal to you but you want to get rid of aches and pains, look into Kelly Starett and Jagroop, they will change your life.

# Thread: High Blood Pressure and CT

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9

### JoanneA — 2012-03-19T01:39:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2511>

I asked Dr. K if CT was ok if you have high blood pressure, he said yes, but since it was on his facebook page i did not want to spam it with anymore questions, can anyone here say more about CT and high blood pressure?

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-19T02:25:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2523>

What kind of questions do you have? I have high blood pressure and I'm doing CT, but I also started on a small dose of blood pressure medicine. I got a home blood pressure cuff and I will be monitoring at home. One goal is to be off high blood pressure meds eventually. My understanding is that to keep eating right, sleep well, do the CT and use a few supplements, monitor a few labs and eventually the system will correct itself.

### JoanneA — 2012-03-19T03:10:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2542>

Im not on meds yet, i guess im afraid of the CT while having HB my last reading yesterday evening was 154/89 im hoping to naturally correct it before meds, my question i guess is reasurance........ Will i be ok doing CT?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-03-19T03:12:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2544>

> **JoanneA;2518 wrote:** I asked Dr. K if CT was ok if you have high blood pressure, he said yes, but since it was on his facebook page i did not want to spam it with anymore questions, can anyone here say more about CT and high blood pressure?

I started the Leptin Rx on Jan 10 and had been Paleo since the first. I started CT on Feb 12. My BP prior to all this was around 125/80 on a BP med. With the eating and CT I have been off all meds for six weeks. I checked my BP before the CT, 15 min into it, and immediately after and it actually lowered mine! So I stopped checking during the CT a few weeks ago and now just do my baths and monitor my BP as usual. Hopefully it will help yours as well.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-19T03:18:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2547>

CT lowers BP eventually......

### JoanneA — 2012-03-19T03:28:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2553>

Well then, it will be another testimony for the forum! Im in!!!

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-19T03:47:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2560>

Joanne, do post your progress. I am eager to get my BP down too, and I'll post my results too.

### Destiny — 2012-03-19T12:53:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-blood-pressure-and-ct.275/post-2677>

My husband lost 30 lbs on the LR. His blood pressure (155/99) went down 3 months after that to 125/78 - the usual reading. It was not immediate so give it a few months. Now, he is doing CT and BP is normal.

### vlynnb — 2012-03-19T18:55:44+0200

# Thread: Hi from CA
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-ca.282/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### karin7771@gmail.com — 2012-03-19T04:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-ca.282/post-2592>

Hi: Trying to figure this all out! I did HCG in the old days when the doctor gave me one shot for the whole month. I ate a lot of meat and veggies in those days just because I like them. Now I'm much older and desire to get my body back in shape. I do like cold water, so that's a plus! Any tips would be greatly appreciated! I did read The Paleo book. I'm ready to start!!

### hellojtm — 2012-04-06T00:00:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-ca.282/post-11934>

Hi Karin,

The starting point is to read a good book on Paleo eating. I found Robb Wolf's book to be awesome and very approachable. But ultimately from a lifestyle perspective I adopted Art DeVany's approach. They eat the same diet and probably live the same way, but it comes across clearer, how to do it forever, in Art's book The New Evolution Diet. But Robb's book is clearer on why not to eat those foods! And Loren Cordain wrote a book called The Paleo Answer that is probably the clearest on the food side but zero lifestyle stuff and not a fun read. So... that's the food. Then Dr. Kruse has a whole meal timing and sleep regimen that is very important to start, but it can be really hard to wade through. It's called the Leptin Rx and there is a forum for it on here and many blog posts. I think it comes down to... eat a paleo diet... eat a high protein breakfast early in the AM... don't snack... don't eat a couple hours before bed... sleep in a completely dark room and don't look at computers/lights for about 2 hours before bed. This should reset a lot of hormonal imbalances that we all suffer from. My suggestion is to go onto the leptin forum and request a Leptin Reset for Dummies post...

best of luck,

jesse

edit: thinking about it, if you just read Robb Wolf's book, and Lights Out by T.S. Wiley you could come back in a year and start on the cold thermogenesis stuff, as long as you really take what you've read to heart.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-08T03:42:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-ca.282/post-12568>

Robb Wolf, plus a keto slanted diet Leptin Rx and CT is the thing you need to slowly adapt your mind too.......Welcome here.

# Thread: Progress... I think?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-03-19T14:59:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-2703>

I've been trucking along pretty well with the CT. The face dunks were very hard for me for about a week and then all of a sudden, it seemed I could keep my face in the water indefinitely. Towards the end of the face dunks I started cooling off the shower and doing a bit of spot icing. About a week and a half ago I started the baths. The first couple were around 58 but since then I've had the bath down to 55 or less. Saturday night I pulled off a 45 minute bath. My longest bath, and would have been longer had my twin 9 month olds not been so unhappy with daddy.

I've been concerned that I've not been seeing any symptoms from doing the CT - not any positive or negative. I do seem to be get used to the cold and tolerating it a lot better. I was quite disappointed I didn't get in early enough last night for my bath.

My husband has also been doing this with me and he had several days where he was VERY fatigued and I assumed it was from the CT. Do I need to be concerned I'm not seeing anything happen yet? Do the nasty die-off side effects I see people writing about come later on? Am I on the right track, or am I missing something?

### vwaggs — 2012-03-19T16:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-2728>

I don't have any major die off symptoms either. But I am not really "sick" at all. Some headaches and tiredness here and there but that is all. I have been doing the baths for 3 weeks every other day and biggest thing I notice is my lack of appetite which is awesome. I am also losing weight where I couldn't before (hCG was the only way I could really lose) and I am on my last 5 lbs. Those last few are always a killer to lose so I am so excited that this is working and I have no hunger what so ever. I couldn't be happier with the results! I know there is "good stuff" happening on the inside even if I can't feel it right now. So I will embrace the fact that I didn't have too much junk inside to die off. I think when people are losing lots of fat, that is when the toxins come out and can make you feel cruddy. I have had a 2-3 day migraine but I am losing fat so I will attribute it to that.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-03-19T17:55:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-2777>

That's the thing... I have plenty of fat to lose! I probably need to lose about 50 pounds. No pounds or inches lost since starting CT. I probably just have to let it work it's magic a little while longer. Anxious to see SOMETHING happen though!

### Phoenix — 2012-03-19T19:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-2812>

> **Trisha;2785 wrote:** That's the thing... I have plenty of fat to lose! I probably need to lose about 50 pounds. No pounds or inches lost since starting CT. I probably just have to let it work it's magic a little while longer. Anxious to see SOMETHING happen though!

My guess, Trisha, is that as a result of those two beautiful 9 month olds, your hormones are still very different than anyone else on this forum. Not too many of us have had twins in the last year! So I don't think you can expect anything "normal" to happen to you as a result of CT. At least not for a while.

Just for the record, today is day 12 for me of CT. I started with cold showers on the 7th, and have worked my way up to cold baths. Just took my 4th yesterday. Nothing for me either. No inches lost, no weight lost. So I'm patiently waiting for something to happen. I'm 55 though ... very different hormone situation that you I would imagine!

Phoenix

### Eve — 2013-03-07T19:40:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79492>

I wanted to bump this to see if anyone else wanted to chime in or if any of the posters here had updates...particularly in terms of weight loss.
I started CT last Spring haphazardly and picked it up again last week after a couple months of hiatus. My CT is the coldest it's ever been as the tap water is cold 42F. Despite the initial shock of getting in the tub, it feels good. I feel energized afterwards and the next day. However, today I feel exhausted and like I need a massage. Is there such thing as delayed detox? No weight loss yet and I have like 30-40lbs to lose

### AK Man — 2013-03-07T21:03:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79504>

You guys may remember me, I have been CT'ing a long time. Here are my long-term observations on CT:

\- It promotes subcutaneous fat growth
\- It is nearly impossible to CT with low functioning thyroid, ie. TSH high/T4 low
\- It is a healthy undertaking in that it boosts metabolism, increases calorie burn, and hopefully leads to BAT expansion
\- BAT is impossible to measure for the average person doing CT
\- Water temp doesn't have to be painfully cold, 50-70 deg F is fine, colder is not needed, warmer does no good
\- CT outdoors is fine if air temps are below 32 deg F, may need longer exposure times, dress just warm enough to prevent frostbite/hypothermia
\- If it gets cold where you live, take advantage of winter temps
\- If it doesn't get cold where you live, seek out the coldest water and swim there as much as you can
\- Consider these: keep house thermostat below 66 degrees, 60 deg at night (or colder if others will allow)
\- CT is not a magic bullet to turn around any disease state, especially involving thyroid
\- CT will not make you slim, but CT in conjunction with eating less and exercising more will
\- Spot icing doesn't work for spot fat reduction
\- You don't need a special diet, ie. keto in winter, to make CT work

### nonchalant — 2013-03-07T21:40:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79516>

Hi AKMan, glad to hear from you again.
I think CT promotes subcutaneous fat in the presence of a large amount of carbs.

### freesia — 2013-03-07T23:35:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79536>

Hi Eve,
I used to get what I called a CT hangover. I think this was while I was shedding some (small amount of) visceral fat, and the resulting estrogen dump. It lasted a couple of months. I eased into CT. I had to because I was pretty unwell when I started. I have been ice bathing consistently every one or two days for 11 months now. All seasons, mid 40s temps for the past 6 months. Overall, no weight loss here either (I wasn't looking for any!). I haven't _noticed_ an increase in subcutaneous fat, but it would make sense to me actually AKman if that did happen.
What did change noticibly was that my waist got smaller - and I put on some muscle.  No exercise. I gained some fitness...and the ability to tolerate change in temperature much better. My body temperature is normal for the first time since adolescence. I feel much fitter and more comfortable (less inflammation) for doing CT. I agree it's not a cure all. Nice tool though if you can manage it...especially for someone like me who had post exertional malaise on trying any kind of exercise.

ETA: Eve I have noticed other benefits from CT too (mentioned in other threads in the CT forum) but have noted observations relating especially to weight/body comp above.
Hope that helps.

### FreeRangeKiwi — 2013-03-08T03:21:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79582>

i think AK's experiences must be highly personalised as they are quite contrary to mine.

i have found that the cooler the water, the more pronounced the effects are mentally - i really enjoy pushing the temp down and find it gets easier in cooler temps - my fat feels like a wetsuit and I feel toasty inside. Not only this, but die off becomes more and more pronounced when the boundaries are pushed. Spot icing has a major impact on both myself and my wife in spot reduction, varicose veins, pain and sleep temp. Keto is imperative for my tolerance and time to see skin blushing (highly recommend eating fish + fat prior).

things started really happening at the 3 week mark for me although i noticed changes after only few sessions. i could sprint what felt like twice as fast as before, would hardly puff, and had an incredible mental improvement (acuity and attitude).

on another note, it is the end of summer here downunder and i am just preparing myself for the change in seasons. i have been a badass and had butter/full dairy instead of ghee for the last month - something I know causes me issues. it seems it has killed my dhea-s/vit-d/cortisol rhythm/circadian clocks as my mood has plunged and my sleep is screwed. i know what to do - the rx's lay it out nicely. but here is the lesson, don't let up. dicing with the grey areas will set you back. you'll find that out as soon as you let up.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-08T03:26:01+0200

### FreeRangeKiwi — 2013-03-08T05:01:34+0200

### freesia — 2013-03-08T05:17:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79602>

FreeRange - so great to hear from someone else about the _fitness gains_! Thanks for sharing.

Progress in that department shocked me...maybe it was so noticable in my case because I was effectively disabled before? I could walk on the flat for 30 - 40 mins but with joint pain (ankles), and post exertional malaise payback. Mornings were the only time I had stamina to stand more than 30 minutes at a time. My progress stunned my husband too because he's fit, trains and plays regular sport...and less than a month of regular ice baths and I could climb a pretty steep hill with less effort than him. What's that about? Seriously.  No puffing either, like you. I'm still shaking my head over that one.
So, with all due respect to AKmans careful observations of his own experience, for _me_ discovering CT has been an extraordinary gift really. The icebaths got me fit and strong enough for the ocean too. Such a long way from where I was a year ago. Perhaps the most obvious advantages are for those with the greatest fitness deficits?? Just speculating there, and trying to make sense of AKmans experience vs mine. It's obviously a personal thing!

I have noticed lots of benefit in those warmer temps too lately. Despite having a small ice making industry going here, and deliveries twice a week too...I can't make the sea colder in summer, so 70 F it is!
Icebaths are still best for me around mid 40's. 42 F yesterday. It's a different beast than a seaswim at 70 F. Couldn't compare the two personally.

AKman.. 66 degree F at home? That sound pretty warm to me. I have 60 degree F and sometimes even less and it feels okay.. not only to me! You get used fast, and I rather put on a bit more clothes than having it warmer. In my bedroom I have it almost as cold as outside = many nights above freezing. I slept all winter long with the widow wide open in any temps, no heating on. But I have warm covers.

### Raiken3712 — 2013-03-08T19:36:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79682>

So having a Keto diet does seem to be important for some people with Cold Adaption.....I guess that means maybe I just need to wait it out. I've been going for about 4 weeks with almost nothing but only 4 days with a low carb diet.

I ate a bit above the 50 carb limit yesterday with some scalloped potatoes...love those things hard to give up...and pork chops with shake n bake....a bit hard to remove that stuff. My mom didn't make any without this time.

Anyway I don't think I got to 100 but it was still a low carb day in comparison to just a week ago when I was binging every 2-3 days.

I'm trying to be strict for a good 2 weeks and those potatoes/seasoned pork chops are my only deviation. Hopefully I'll notice something next week. I think I might be getting a bit better...maybe but its hard to tell.

I do seem to respond positively in regards to energy levels afterwards, but its very temporary...like the cold just energizes me...doesn't last long.

I'm very curious about the Thyroid issue....according to one of the CT posts...CT bypasses the Thyroid or something...is this based on faulty reasoning or is there some reason it doesn't happen in everyone? If the Thyroid hormones being screwed messes this up than if I have issues maybe I can't get the most out of it without fixing the issue.

The Thyroid bypass mechanism if it works at all like that should allow adaption even with a Thyroid problem....I would think.

"THE COLD ADAPTED HUMAN THYROID FXN: does not bother with T3 at all. Why? When you are supremely LS by cold you go straight the the source, the hypothalamus and make TRH from the brain. The brain controls all thyroid function in coldâ€¦â€¦.forget the Moose thyroid.

SKEPTIC BOMB: You bypass all hormones and TSH too. TRH drives the whole show. The brain is completely in control and it up regulates fat burning everywhere. This is how the Ancient Pathway lights your pilot light. The warm adapted human always complains about the cold and always feels coldâ€¦â€¦the cold adapted on is always pink to cherry in cold radiating heat like a furnace. You can thank TRH for this. This is does not even require a thyroid gland either. Is not life grand in the cold, folks?"

"Once leptin enters and binds to its receptors, it effects the lateral hypothalamic tracts to immediately send a second messenger signal to the thyroid to signal it to up-regulate thyroid function and efficiency. See whne we are warm adapted it require help to partially access this pathway from T3 hormone. If you are LR you never can access this pathway. This specifically is how we can raise our basal metabolic rate when we are leptin sensitive. These coupled events, matched with leptinâ€™s actions peripherally in muscles, occur at the UCP3 sitesâ€¦â€¦to burn fat as we sleep at a higher basal metabolic rate. This means electron chain transport does not make ATP as usual. When leptin allows this uncoupling to occur we make heat and not energy from normal metabolism. This means we will burn off our excess calories as pure heat."

This part leads me to believe perhaps the reason I'm not seeing many of the effects is because my Leptin level is still high because of my constant binges. I have to have enough days of rather low carb before my Leptin levels fall....perhaps 2 weeks is the amount of time it takes for the cold to reduce Leptin sufficiently?

I'm guessing this varies depending on how many carbohydrates you eat...would love some feedback on my thoughts.

Thanks

Brain isn't well fueled enough...still a slow thinker...probably because of my deficiencies up till now. The Fluoride in the drinking water and other toxins probably haven't helped.

I'm currently drinking RO water that I get periodically when it runs out in the 5 gallon jugs I fill to put on my water dispenser. Wonder how much Fluoride I get from my food since sadly I have a mostly conventional food base without much wild caught fish and not any pastured grass-fed animals. Though they are still probably dosed with water that contains Fluoride...eating their proper diet probably leads to a nutrient profile which would assist with Fluoride...perhaps they get more Iodine.

I'm somewhat curious about Iodine in cattle...they get it from bugs accidentally eaten and its in the grass to a certain extent? I wonder if our animals get enough Iodine when being pastured. I'd love to see a comparison of more than just the Omega 3/Omega 6 with pastured animals vs Feedlot. There aren't enough nutritional comparisons out there involving stuff like that...I love numbers oh well.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-08T19:57:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progress-i-think.287/post-79684>

> [Raiken3712 said:](/goto/post?id=79682)
>
> I'm somewhat curious about Iodine in cattle...they get it from bugs accidentally eaten and its in the grass to a certain extent? I wonder if our animals get enough Iodine when being pastured. I'd love to see a comparison of more than just the Omega 3/Omega 6 with pastured animals vs Feedlot. There aren't enough nutritional comparisons out there involving stuff like that...I love numbers oh well.

I believe coastal hay is prized for livestock. Maybe it's the higher iodine content?

I'm confused also about the thyroid and CT. I'm pretty low on thyroid, though with iodine supplementation it is edging upward. But adapting to CT has never been a problem for me. I didn't hurry through the adaptation, but I seemed to adapt in an exponential curve. Especially below 50F. So "bypassing the thyroid" seems to apply to me. But there are plenty of others with low thyroid that experience considerable difficulty with CT. Perhaps it is a protein uncoupling issue?

I don't think you have to have a strict keto diet, but heavy on the animal fats and proteins, definitely.

# Thread: Chapped Lips?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chapped-lips.288/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### edance83@yahoo.com — 2012-03-19T16:09:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chapped-lips.288/post-2729>

This isn't necessarily from the Reset or CT as I have been having problems with chapped lips before I was even Paleo. I have them year round no matter the season. I put coconut oil on them a lot, but it only helps for a moment. Does anyone know what vitamin or mineral I may be lacking that could cause persistently chapped lips?

I know mouth breathing can also be a cause and I have been trying to consciously keep my mouth closed more and breath through my nose. It doesn't seem to make much of a difference so far.

### edance83@yahoo.com — 2012-03-20T15:44:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chapped-lips.288/post-3223>

No one has any ideas?

### PrimalPam — 2012-03-21T00:39:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chapped-lips.288/post-3588>

I had terrible chapped lips for a while last year. I did a ton of searches for some soothing, because normal lip balms did not seem to help. So here's what I did (and am doing now, as they are a bit chapped again): At night before bed: Apply Aquaphor around the edges of your lips, especially the areas which are cracked. You can find it in the lotions section at the drugstore. Then apply Blistex medicated (the one in the small blue tub) on the rest of your lips. Then apply Vaseline around the edges to prevent the Aquaphor from migrating. I use this formula every time my lips get chapped, and they are usually better by morning.

During the day: apply Vaseline liberally.

### Maria12345 — 2025-09-05T05:49:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chapped-lips.288/post-359149>

> [edance83@yahoo.com said:](/goto/post?id=2729)

> This isn't necessarily from the Reset or CT as I have been having problems with chapped lips before I was even Paleo. I have them year round no matter the season. I put coconut oil on them a lot, but it only helps for a moment. Does anyone know what vitamin or mineral I may be lacking that could cause persistently chapped lips?

Did you ever find out why they are chapped, and did you find a solution? I have the exact same thing right now.

### Julz — 2025-09-05T16:25:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chapped-lips.288/post-359153>

Mine got better after consuming a lot more fish

### Jack Kruse — 2025-09-05T19:00:58+0300

# Thread: Details, Details, how are you performing your CT to get skin 50-55 degrees.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 213
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-19T17:25:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2761>

I'm trying to figure out ways to spend more time in CT, like ice suit so I can clean house and cook those paleo meals all while CTing.

I just did dishes and spent lots of time adjusting falling homemade gelpacks and generally thinking "there must be a better way". Please share hints and tricks.

I did make my own gel bags with 4/1 ratio of water to alcohol. Those are good. Getting them to stay in place isn't so good.

I'd love a "CT ROBE" or something. Any ideas? Anyone in the Boulder area want to partner on manufacturing something and making an awesome product we can then sell of so we can buy a fun estate in the mountains?

ok, so now I am sandwiching gel bags between the compression garmet against my skin, and one on top. HOwever these do not lend themselves to moving around.

Ideas?!

### BJK77 — 2012-03-19T18:44:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2797>

I've been trying to create my own little homemade ice vest with duct tape . . . quite the sight! It isn't working so well though. I'd love any ideas too. I struggle to find the time to lay down or sit on the couch for 45-60 minutes a day with the ice packs on. It's difficult enough to get my bath time in as frequently as I'd like.

### ealachan — 2012-03-19T18:49:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2802>

Could you bind the gel packs to your body with an Ace bandage, maybe?

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-19T19:02:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2811>

I've been wondering about how to fit CT into my schedule also, and then I think that maybe the problem is one we've created ourselves with our neolithic brains. Maybe one of the points of this is to stop trying to multi-task and just embrace the CT for what it's supposed to be - an ancient pathway to health.

Of course that being said, we all live in a modern world where there just isn't enough time for anything. Personally, if I'm sitting down (at home, having done it at work yet), I've got an ice pack on/underneath something. I've also tried this in the car to/from work even though that's only a 15 min commute one way. I've been tucking things in shirts or wrapping with ace bandages to hold against the skin. Not perfect, but cheap and effective.

### tweety — 2012-03-19T19:38:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2835>

my husband found a clay therma-paq thing at target for $20. I haven't used it while doing activities, but am about to try. I put it on my tummy last night while reading. I try to read or do something calming before bed most nights so I am implementing it then. I would like to find a morning use for it though

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-19T19:42:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2838>

> **tweety;2844 wrote:** my husband found a clay therma-paq thing at target for $20. I haven't used it while doing activities, but am about to try. I put it on my tummy last night while reading. I try to read or do something calming before bed most nights so I am implementing it then. I would like to find a morning use for it though

That reminded me, tweety. I've got some of those freezable bubble-pack things from Walmart that I've slept on a night or two when I didn't have time for a bath. They don't have condensation and are comfortable enough to fall asleep on since I'm using them on a mattress. Since I sleep on my stomach and I need the most CTing there, it's worked out pretty well. Just an idea.

### JC2K — 2012-03-19T21:06:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2888>

I got this vest at Amazon. Substantially cheaper than Arctic Heat, and has more ice coverage, adjustable. A bit bulky though, not sure I'd want to roam around much with it either stylewise.

[http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Ice-Vest-Navy/dp/B001P30358/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1332183843&sr=8-1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Ice-Vest-Navy/dp/B001P30358/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1332183843&sr=8-1)

You can stick your own ice blocks in it too, since it is velcroed pockets. I like it so far.

### lynn@ellijay.com — 2012-03-19T21:09:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2890>

Those bubble-ice pack things from Walmart are really great. They don't get you wet and it is easy to tuck them under garments. I bought a cheap "Spanks" type under-thing that comes up high on my waist. I tuck one bubble pack around my tummy (under the Spanks) and the second one I run up my back, tucked under a sports bra (the bottom part tucks into the shorts too.) It is easy to do your housework or whatever while you are icing. And my skin gets under 50 degrees under the ice pack but they have not left me with a bruise or freezer burn. Look for some - they are $2.88 at my store and they re-freeze quickly.

### Snowyky — 2012-03-19T21:15:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2897>

I made one using those waist trimmer belts ([http://www.walmart.com/ip/Gold-s-Gym-Waist-Trimmer-Belt/12167842](http://http://www.walmart.com/ip/Gold-s-Gym-Waist-Trimmer-Belt/12167842)) I'm a quilter so I have mega fabric around, I bought a couple of the belts, so cheap, under $5. Cut them then sewed them to material I had sewn into a long enough 'belt' to go around my body with pockets that I then put in a couple of those ice sheets ([http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Refreezable-Sheets-pack/dp/B001PTGO5A](http://http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Refreezable-Sheets-pack/dp/B001PTGO5A)), also made small ones to go around the top part of the arms, could make some to go around the thighs but I find that the legs get enough from the ice baths, don't really need them. [Here is a link to the image](http://https://www.facebook.com/media/set/?set=a.10150650516843702.400203.726593701&type=1), I also use it in the tub to hold the bag of ice, which I let out part of the ice so it forms around my body easier, attach the velcro so it's wider at the top then the bottom and it lets me move around but keeps the ice in place. The smaller ones have pockets big enough for the pint size ice blocks, but it's easier to use if you use the ice sheets, fits better.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-03-19T23:08:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2947>

For spot CTing I have two large ziplock bags I made into ice packs. I wear a compression shirt and then put a "spanx" type shirt on over it. Then I tie two scarves around my waist. I keep wondering if my neighbors can see in the windows in the morning when I'm stuffing my shirt with ice blocks. I checked myself in the mirror and I look like a sumo wrestler.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-19T23:28:53+0200

### blindpig — 2012-03-19T23:44:07+0200

### Mom — 2012-03-19T23:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2961>

I just took an old all-over-control bathing suit that is too big for me now (yippee!) and cut a line in the lining just below the bust. I put the icepacks inside between the 2 layers of the suit. I put my clothes on over the bathing suit so if UPS rings the bell I am able to answer the door looking relatively normal. I think this will work for me for a while. It actually would be more comfortable if the bathing suit wasn't as baggy.

### AKMan — 2012-03-20T01:00:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-2990>

So, being in a hotel in sunny Florida, I had to do something. I raided the ice machine down the hallway, filled a trashbag with ice, and watched Nat Geo's new show on Arctic Life.

This is the first time I've done ice bags, but I think it worked pretty good. I kept moving it around when a spot got too uncomfortable. It would turn my skin a nice cherry red after about 5 minutes. I didn't bring my thermometer, but my skin got pretty cold.

From all the studies I've read, spot treatments should be a fine way to activate the brown fat. Most of the research they do involves putting people's feet in cold water (40-50 deg), so a bag of ice should definitely give your brain a signal that you are exposed to cold.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-20T02:25:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-3026>

Just got to 60 minutes with a 20lb bag of ice on a compression shirt and need to start without the shirt tonight.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-20T02:43:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-3037>

Ok, the seal n save thing has piqued my interest!! I could do a LONG homemade ice blanket made of the alcohol/water mix. Shouldn't leak much unlike the baggies. perhaps even superduper long and could hang them over shoulders. . . then I could wrap a robe around tight to make em stick against my body. . . .I need a way to seal it out of the machine. Side of an iron? That way I could make it like baffles.

Love the bathing suit idea. I'll have to go find a 1 piece, I've been tank-ini-ing and all my ice keeps falling out when I'm moving around.

I so have to start taking photos of this stuff. Do you ever just kind of wonder/imagine that we might all be nuts and in the throes of a cult?

Did some major ice package today. Totally easy to do. I had been doing face dunks, and showers of a 1-1.5 min duration. Had no problem doing 45 mins of ice bags.

Love the ideas.

I was in 62 degree water and my skin got to 65 degrees, but never turned even slightly pink. Sounds like some of y'all are getting pink from this. Does that mean my 5:1 O6:3 ratio is actually serving me well, or is somethign broken in me?

### JC2K — 2012-03-20T05:09:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-3121>

> **Cavemam;2973 wrote:** Oooh, thanks. Have you worn it for long periods (30mins - 60mins). Does it stay frozen long enough to keep skin temps down for a long haul.

I've been wearing it around 45 min with a t-shirt under it. It actually gets a bit uncomfortable by then so I take it off. It gets my skin temp down to the 50's at the end (testing with an IR thermometer). There are a bunch of small ice blocks in it, and depending on where it was touching the body, some blocks are more melted than others, so it's hard to judge. The Amazon site has people saying it lasts 2 hours, but that's not quite what we're looking for. I'm guesstimating it will last an hour easy, but not two hours for the temp we want.

It gives me full coverage front and most of the back.

### AKMan — 2012-03-20T12:50:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-3178>

> **MamaGrok;3128 wrote:** Love the ideas.
>
>
>
> I was in 62 degree water and my skin got to 65 degrees, but never turned even slightly pink. Sounds like some of y'all are getting pink from this. Does that mean my 5:1 O6:3 ratio is actually serving me well, or is somethign broken in me?

I have been filling a plastic bag with ice cubes, maybe 2 quarts worth, and holding it on my belly and legs til it gets about numb--a couple minutes in each place anyway. This will turn the area under it a nice cherry red, which lasts 5-10 minutes. try that and see what happens. Cold air and cold water don't turn me red, either. I keep doing this until the ice is about melted, which can be an hour or more. It's easy to do while watching TV. It really gets cold when you get more water than ice, more skin contact.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-03-20T13:36:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/details-details-how-are-you-performing-your-ct-to-get-skin-50-55-degrees.290/post-3181>

Just ordered an IR thermometer to check skin temperature - I've been using my old-style mercury under-the-tongue one which is accurate in bathwater but useless for skin temperature! I've been cold showering for 5 - 10 minutes every morning, having a cold bath at about 56F for about 20 - 30 minutes a day, and using picnic ice-blocks for spot CT for a couple of hours every evening. I only do my tummy, and I move the block around as patches get cold and uncomfortable. By the end of the evening my tummy is bright red and really cold to the touch, and takes 3 - 4 hours to heat up again! I had a look at the cooling vests on Amazon UK, but they are extremely expensive, so I think I'll stick with what I'm doing for the time-being, and maybe think again once the thermometer turns up.
