### MamaGrok — 2012-03-22T17:15:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4432>

So, I don't know much about interpreting charts for infertility, but some questions that come to mind ... is it normal for the temp to rise so slowly? And go down so gradually pre-cycle? If you go to nfpandmore.org, that's the website of Sheila & John Kippley, who founded the CCLI, and are the reason that any of us even know about NFP/FAM today. I've found Sheila to be wonderfully open to helping interpret charts ... I wouldn't hesitate to send her the chart and see if anything stands out to her.

> **PaleoCowgirl;4182 wrote:** I don't plan to have a baby for a couple of years, which is probably a good thing since I'm not currently cycling at all. Just got off hbc in Feb. and nothing yet... but suddenly, I'm getting the urge to WANT/NEED to cycle and WANT/NEED to carry a baby. Is that illogical? At any rate, I'm learning a ton from all of you before I take that big step.
>
>
>
> Mamagrok, how does the breastfeeding work in your case for birth control? I know a lot of women who have had oopsie babies after thinking that breastfeeding would keep them from getting pregnant.
>
>
>
> Bee, good luck with your babe on the way! Keep us posted on how you're doing!

Sorry, I missed this post till it was quoted! I keep finding that the "next new post" button seems to skip a few posts. Anyway, um, yes, why would it be illogical for your body to ask you to do something that it expects should have happened about 7 years ago? Lol, we don't do this anymore, but our bodies really do expect to start menstruating around 16 and start having babies soon after, one every 3 years or so, till we're about 45. So I wouldn't be surprised at all to find that kind of reaction once your hormones are restored.

Ecological breastfeeding is an amazing, beautiful thing, but it's a lifestyle more than just a number of times per day or something like that. My sister was conceived three months after I was born, so the only thing I ever knew about bf'ing and child spacing was "It's a myth. It doesn't work at all!"

Well, my children have all been spaced about 2.5-3.5 years apart by nothing other than bf'ing. But again, it's not just "on demand," and it's not just "exclusive." (Although I was successful in ecobf'ing my first, without knowing what it was, and while I was working - but she nursed ALL NIGHT while we both slept, and I pumped several times a day.) It's a relationship where mother & baby are together and the baby just pokes around and nurses whenever, without the mother saying, "but you just nursed!" any more than she would say, "but you just kissed me!" This is seen in every traditional society I've ever heard of, some more than others (the !Kung tribe of Africa was found to be nursing every 4 minutes, for just a few seconds ... obviously babywearing helps with this!). It keeps the prolactin level high - frequency is the key, as in the title of Sheila's recent book The Frequency Factor. There is so much on this subject, and one day I want to blog on it, b/c it's so misunderstood ... it DOES work, but not every time in this modern world. It's in our bodies, but there are physical and situational factors that make it difficult. Even without the spacing results, though, everything you do that would encourage it in an ideal world, is still good for mother and baby. All of it.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-22T17:54:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4459>

Sling. Wraps. It's a way of life; ask the billions of women who preceded us, maintaining a household, garden, cooking, raising older children, and ... nursing the bay!

One thing I've noticed as my fatigue has grown over the last few years is that I don't feel like nursing while working anymore. With the last two kids, I had to choose, and I decided nursing was more important to me, so I sat (and used the computer, usually) while nursing. I wanted to sit, anyway. I think this (hypothyroidism?) undiagnosed chronic fatigue is probably a huge reason why a lot of women feel hands-free nursing while ggoing about everyday tasks is undesirable and/or impossible.

Come on, hormonal reversal!! (Also, nursing while sleeping became impossible with the last baby. Because it was so second-nature before, I believe with no doubt that this is a result of my worsening health, and probably specificalyl estrogen dominance. I know this is a problem for a lot of modern women, too.)

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-22T18:00:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4465>

@PaleoCowgirl...MamaG's got all the info on bfing and spacing!

my kids are exactly 2yrs apart not planned either! w/dd I was pumping - never made enough, had to supplement w/formula... aroudn 14mths my supply dried up (I just couldn't keep pumping w/out medications & herbal supplements) I was pregnant a month later w/o a cycle - but I'd been O'ing since 8mths postpartum - you can tell when you you O, when pumping b/c you see your supply drop...

W/ds I got my cycle back ~15mths pp (which was 1mth after being on the LR) ds is still nursing, though I've cut him back to mornings/evenings only. W/him... he nursed non-stop when I wasn't at work. even at 15mths he was nursing something like 8x/day - which in our society - that's not \*normal\* for a 15mth old toddler...but it was normal for him.... Its only been w/in the pat 6mths that he himself has cut back... and I'm actually interested in dropping his morning session - but will wait until he stops asking to nurse when I pick him up from daycare... when he stops asking for that - then I'll move to drop the am session... I figure he'll nurse to sleep for a while longer

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-22T23:21:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4653>

> **MamaGrok;4363 wrote:** And I don't know about temps; I never keep a thermometer working and by the bed long enough to catch on to the patterns, lol. I really do need a glass thermometer; do they sell the mercury ones anymore?

LOL - My now progressive lens glasses, my BD digital and my Geratherm NON MERCURY glass therms sit on the night stand. Alarm clock has 2 alarms in it set 6 mins apart. I get up, turn off the first, climb back in to bed, insert therms and drift back to sleep. Amazing how that 5 mins between the digital beeping and the glass one finishing is just enough to put me back under. I just leave them on the night stand and the dh picks up the data. I also have chart inside my med cabinet where I write down NOTES - like drank red wine last night, woke up to screaming kid, and anything else that can alter the temps.

I had to order my glass thermometer on line. I had a really really old mercury one from the beginning of our marriage... it finally broke.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-22T23:44:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4667>

> **L8F;4400 wrote:** I have read that you can get the mercury therms at antique shops or I imagine ebay etc...I think they are not allowed to be sold anymore. Bummer for temping. I hate my current digital one. Will shop around.
>
>
>
> Yes, I think the infertility epidemic will have people looking at hormones more closely, but might just lead down the assisted fertility avenue until that don't work anymore...depressing, but I guess it got me to look at my health in a really different way.
>
>
>
> Chart photo test, from first cycle sans cramps, started LR Jan 13, this is start date Jan 29:
>
>
>
>
> [](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/4400=22-chart Feb 12.jpg>)

s

Very nice chart...nice and neat. The things I notice is that the full temp shift - the temps elevated .4 above the previous 6 comes late - no surprise given the issues you describe. Also it seems interesting that you seem to come down almost immediately and then spike again right at TOTM. BUT since you started LRx in the middle of a cycle I would definitely not stress about anything I see here. I'm not sure how this software works, but can you superimpose multiple charts on top of each other? My dh does that for me all the time in excel and I actually have a trend line running through the graph which is a simple average of all the previous cycles... so there is a faint line that allows me to quickly see how far off I am from a "usual day 14". If you can't do it with the software... we can certainly start a super simple excel table and plot this with the next few months all together and see if things start shifting.

And while I know TTC is a big part of the picture here, health has to be #1 priority. You mentioned the quality of oocytes being diminished by our hormonal status. NO kidding!! I am certain that my son's de novo chromosonal deletion was helped by my lousy state of health at the time we got pregnant. NB we had several months where we were right on time/target to conceive and we didn't. Not exactly our history. And IF I had been paying attention I would have seen these misses - which were probably 2 week spontaneous aborts as a warning sign that all was not well with me or my baby making house. Every child is a blessing, but if I knew then what I know now, I know I could have given that little guy a much better start in life. I would have delayed TTC until I was at a better weight and looking much better on chart paper.

So here's to more ice, more temps, more fat in the mouth/less on the hips, more data, and lots of fun trying.

### L8F — 2012-03-23T00:48:54+0200

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-23T01:04:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4706>

> **L8F;4719 wrote:** I looked at some of those sites, and was kind of bummed that there had to be a class for the one that seemed like best option for couples. Can you imagine a young couple going to a class about cervical mucus. My husband would have to be in a coma to go to that!!! Lucky that your husband is involved.

The thought of CCLI (Couple to Couple League International) is that family planning is a job for the couple, not just the woman. So it is taught as a class, and it is supposed to be the woman's job to take the temps and the husband's job to do the charting (& often he'll do any software parts & manage the thermometer, waking the wife if she wants the temp at a certain time every day, etc.), and the couple's job to do the chart interpreting and discerning whether this month they are feeling called to achieve or avoid pregnancy.

That's the ideal, LOL, it doesn't always happen that way. But that's the way it worked in our marriage, and sounds like it in cgk's, too. I love the beauty of the teamwork; it's not the woman's burden. And I can tell you that every man I've ever known to take an NFP class has come out with an amazing appreciation of his wife's body, the gift of her fertility, and a renewed appreciation of her hormonal complexity, lol!

It's usually not an easy sell, but many Catholic dioceses make it a requirement for marrying in the Church, so it doesn't always have to be sold, lol. The classes are always open to those of any or no faith, though, and I think there would be only gain to anyone who managed to sell her hubby on it!

### L8F — 2012-03-23T01:30:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4727>

Hmm, I wish we had had to do that, but at the time, I wonder if I would have even been interested. Not attaining a goal does wonders to open up a mind. I hemmed and hawed about pre cana, but ended up liking it a lot.

Most of our relationship and marriage has been apart due to medical training issues, and we have just been living together for over a year. Teamwork is still a work in progress cause we are on weirdo schedules. I will show him your post. I have already told him about the gluten free communion that you discussed since I thought he might find it interesting.

I also think the charting becomes more of a couples issue when doing it correctly means more sex, and/or more spacing between babies. That is likely excellent motivation for a guy to get on board asap if he is interested in either of those two ideas. For us there are no restrictions x 4 years or so, so I think he might not see the point exactly..

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-23T02:38:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4764>

Yes, yes we need to get Mr. L8F totally into this. Not sure how to do that, but I will add it to my prayer list. And no you don't need to take him with you to go to a class. Call the Diocese and find out where the classes are given. If your parish has a permanent deacon - an ordained married man - find him. They are the BEST with helping find resources. If your husband is devout, you could also ask him to take on reading a bit about how his faith and fertility go together. It could be a different sort of Lenten sacrifice - one that would certainly bear good fruit.

So I had the plague this month - fevers, heaves, sinus disaster - all of it. Missed the typical window for my LH surge... not that either of us - he got the plague too - was all that interested in anything other than codeine cough syrup. So surge finally 2 days ago CD 17 instead of 12-14. And there is my knight in shining armor standing with the steak to grill, wine in hand telling me about how if you look at the last X number of cycles my temp 2 days after surge was in the range of y and z. And despite the surge being late, my temp this am was dead on where it should be 2 days post surge. Then he went on to tell me that we seem to be trending that my 3 days of elevated temps + 4 days of drying up seem to come about 4 days past LH surge, even though the LH surge typically means we would be "in the clear" within 72 hrs. And that sets my mood all in the right direction

Unquestionably, I would say he is motivated by more sex, but that is how he genuinely feels loved - physically. Doesn't matter if it is little affections - hugs, kisses, or the wild thing - he's a guy and that is how he's wired. So when I handed him a detailed document from one Diocese how to figure out how to navigate perimenopause, he read it. He knows that managing this well means taking care of me, my fragile moods, and my anxious heart. No I don't think I've ever withheld relations from him over any of this, but he knows the more he is into it, the more we can truly enjoy the creative power that shared fertility is.

There is another really good book to read by Christopher West - Good News about Sex and Marriage

[http://www.barnesandnoble.com/w/good-news-about-sex-and-marriage-christopher-west/1004087509](http://http://www.barnesandnoble.com/w/good-news-about-sex-and-marriage-christopher-west/1004087509)

It is a very current talk about the physical and spiritual dimensions of sex in a marriage. Not sure how readily it would be in a public library, but ask that deacon... It is a book that really eliminates the idea that Christian sex is prudish, and yet has an amazing way of elevating it way beyond the mix of passion and hormones. Plus it answers questions you would never want to breathe aloud during a pre cana meeting

And lastly, I always tell couples who are struggling with fertility to pray a little differently. Instead of praying for a baby, pray for a new priest or a religious from your family. When we ask for children, and the ensuing grandchildren we are asking for a gift to us that is indeed good. But when you pray for a vocation from your family, you are immediately offering that child back to God - who really only loans these little ones to us. God is never, ever outdone in generosity - that is theologically impossible. Pray that His will be done and that you are overwhelmed with the grace to cooperate with it and be full of peace.

okay - need to spend some quality time with chart man!

### thyme — 2012-03-23T12:55:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4895>

Can I throw myself on your sympathy for a bit, being in that time and stage of month in which I am spending a bit too much time each day whacking myself in the boobs to see if they still hurt?

This is my first month charting, first time trying to conceive, and I'm 36 years old. I had a non-hormonal, copper IUD removed in early January, and my formerly-boringly-normal-28-day-lunar-synced cycles went bonkers after that; this cycle, so far, feels normal, finally. My partner had his vasectomy reversed in late January. His pipes are now working and a good number of fast swimmers are popping through (We've used our home microscope to peep, which is really cool!!), but the doc says it often takes 6 months for post VR numbers to come all the way up; between that and our relatively advanced ages for doing this sort of thing, we're figuring we get at least that many months of practice before worrying about tadpoles settling in.

E.g.--although I very much want to conceive, I'm truly not anticipating it happening for another few months, so I'm pretty sure that whatever is hijacking my body's hormonal systems right now is not entirely hallucinatory.

But man, there's something going on there. The temp spike on Day 21 was huge--my partner told me in the middle of the night that I was burning up; I could feel the sweat pouring off me, and then I woke up with BOOBS. I couldn't tell if they were actually larger than usual (they're large to start with) but they were THERE in a way that they have never been before. I could just...feel them.

By day 23, the boobs weren't so noticeable but the nipples hurt--as in, hugging my partner was seriously painful in the morning. They're less sensitive now, but still far more so than normal.

On day 24 & 25, hit weird walls of fatigue in the late afternoon. We were working in the garden and I just...couldn't...work...anymore.

Early response pee stick this morning, day 27, negative. Ditto the one yesterday, and the day before that. (There might or might not have been more than one pee stick involved on any given day because I'm not suddenly finding myself compulsive or anything.)

So questions:

1) I can't tell if I'm likely to have ovulated on day 14 or day 20--can you? I don't trust my CM readings yet--I need another cycle to understand the elusive difference between eggwhite and watery, so that's not helpful. Ditto on ferning--I didn't see it at any time, and still don't, and I am not convinced it is not a plot to drive me bonkers.

2) If this isn't a conception cycle, then what's happening? Is this boob/high temp thing part of a normal cycle that I've never experienced before? Could this be CT madness? I've been doing tub CT since mid-Feb, about 50F; I was fairly well adapted by the end of Feb. well (I live in an unheated Scottish house, so I think I had a head start.) I've been following the LR-x since early Feb; I think I'm LS, but I really like my BAB.

3) Relative odds of this being a conception cycle? I'm more than okay with the idea of the tadpole swimming away again before it makes its presence known on a pee stick, but it'd be something to know that our respective bits & pieces are actually working correctly...or that I've gone completely mental.

thank you!! (thyme from MDA; MG, I'm still testing a couple of nightshade-less curries for you--I didn't like my first iteration)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/4926=30-thyme3.jpg)

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-23T13:24:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4901>

Thyme - things are definitely going to be wonky when you come off an IUD. Sure it is non hormonal, but that doesn't mean your body doesn't respond to the change. And BTW, if you are indeed pregnant off your first chart, join the club! It took me awhile to learn to chart because I had very few months where I wasn't pregnant in the first several years.

My experience with adding LRx & CT to my life has been to have a more steep temp rise. Instead of trolling along and creeping my way up over 4-6 days and getting there by 21, I'm there by 19. But the temp shift indicates that ovulation has already occured. So while your temperatures are shifted - 3 days .4 above the previous 6 by day 21, ovulation occured previous to that. AND since you don't have all your temps from the first part of the cycle here it is possible that you had your shift earlier. So hard to say. Also, when I blow up the chart, it gets too distorted to read, so I can't see where you all had relations.

Give it a couple more days. You should see your temps come down if you aren't pregnant. It is also quite possible that LRx, CT, and removing the IUD can make this cycle just plain longer - but by day 35 you should have more data and a more likely answer. That said, you should see your temps get back as low as at the beginning of this chart - usually by day 3 of the next cycle. If I come down quickly (crashing) my PMS symptoms are usually more dramatic. If I coast down slowly, they are usually more tolerable. Don't drive yourself crazy with the pregnancy tests. Stay reasonably well hydrated through out the day and just test that once in the morning when you wake up. And then let it rest. Cortisol levels that are banging high off the ceiling are good for no woman - pregnant or NOT!

Getting comfortable with CM can take a few months. You just have to get familiar with it by comparing what you see from one day to the next. Don't worry it will come. Before you know it you will be able to discern EWCM from seminal residue.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-23T13:52:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4906>

> **Jennifer;4416 wrote:** I've had Hashimotos for years and the Endo signed off on me years ago. PMD doesn't check anything but TSH, free T4 and the usual. But I must admit that I haven't even thought about retesting antibodies. But not a bad idea considering all the chaos I went thru with my thyroid when I went gluten free. I didn't know about Gluten- Hashimotos connection when I went GF. Then I started feeling dizzy and my hair failing out so I asked for my TSH To be checked and it was high. My doc overreacted and took me off meds. Then I was hypothyroid. Now I'm on half the dose I used to be. But all that took months so goodness only knows how much damage was done.
>
> As for my cortisol. I haven't had that tested in years but I was in adrenal fatigue in 2009. I think I know my adrenals are hurting but have been afraid to see the Endo because I don't want him to try up put me on steroids. So I am doing Leptin Reset and CT and praying I can heal myself. Naive or breaking the paradigm? Not sure yet.

I don't have a whit of experience with Hashi's so I'm learning it all from women out here, but Jennifer I think you are doing the right thing trying to heal this all naturally. That said, I would put the LRx in ahead of CT unless you feel your diet has been really good for a while. CT does seem to bring out the best and the worst all at the same time. If the diet is dialed in better, you might have less hormonal bumps going down the CT road.

I wouldn't throw in the towel and admit early onset menopause yet by any means. I've seen too much improvement to my own situation - badly bumping along through perimenopause - with getting LS, that I think it merits giving this a try. That all said, I think of LR as an over arching problem that masks other issues and compounds them. So taking it off the table by getting LS will help see what else might be genuinely broken and need help with hormonal intervention or some other therapy.

There is a website called womentowomen.com that is a group in Maine that tries to work these issues naturally. They sell supplements which always gives me pause - particularly since they aren't custom blended for each patient, but a group of different "formulas" They also shun ketogenic diets. That all said, they do have quite a bit of good reading on thyroid/adrenal/sex hormone relationships that seems to be legit. They do have some recent articles about early onset menopause, that might be worth reading - if nothing else to see if you genuinely fit the profile or if there is something that just needs fixing.

Yay! Buffalo... I'm originally from Rochester. Nice long natural CT season up there!!

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-23T14:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-4912>

My glass thermometer

[http://www.amazon.com/Geratherm-Basal-Mercury-Free-Thermometer/dp/B0013NB6CY](http://http://www.amazon.com/Geratherm-Basal-Mercury-Free-Thermometer/dp/B0013NB6CY)

To address some of the "complaints" in the reviews

Yes, the shape is broad, but ladies, it is still slimmer than a junior tampon.

Yes, it can be difficult to shake down. It doesn't necessarily go with a flick of the wrist. It is more of a shake from the shoulder.

No it isn't difficult to read, once you know you are looking for a blue line

I don't use the magnfying case to read it - but common sense would say rinse off the therm in cold water if you do, so CM doesn't travel to the case.

This isn't a mercury thermometer, so the cold water doesn't make the fluid contract. The temp stays there until you shake it down. Nice so you can just take the temp and leave it on the bed table until you actually get out of bed and read it.

As I mentioned before, I play lab rat for most of this. Temps are where you can get the spouse most involved. So I just produce the heat and let him do the rest. I, on the other hand do all the work with the CM.

### hazyjane — 2012-03-25T02:08:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-5626>

> **L8F;4338 wrote:** The most interesting part of IVF and other fertility treatments like Clomid is that they didn't work for me. I already ovulate, but cannot sustain a pregnancy. I now put it together like this:
>
> I have never had a LP defect in terms of short LP, but I do have pan low hormones stemming originally from low total cholesterol, as well as hash's. So...no matter what they made my ovaries do on command, like ovulate a bunch, it didn't matter if I didn't have the building blocks for all the hormones I needed, and even exogenous hormones end, and my body would have to take over. From a brief discussion with JK on low cholesterol on his blog comments, he discussed that cholesterol is needed for cell division to make the mitotic spindles, and this makes a whole lot of sense to me. If I have no building materials, forcing my body to make a million eggs will just lead to me having a bunch of eggs that cannot get to where they need to go.
>
>
>
> Looking at my whole hormone cascade made SO much more sense to me than what the REI docs look at which is E and P, and a screen to make sure you don't have high T. They also look at a lot of antibodies and clotting factors, all of which they note as problems, but have no effective solutions other than immunosuppressants etc. My aha moment was when I recalled a phrase my doc said in the office. He noted anti TPOs (Hashi's) and said something like "That is no big deal, it just means synthroid down the line"
>
>
>
> At the time I thought this was ok too. In retrospect one day I started researching Hashi's and what it meant, and found it to be an independent factor for pregnancy loss, and thus I ended up with the LRx! Happy outcome to a formerly bleak situation. So..I take my diet VERY seriously and cheat with wheat for nothing or no one.

With Hashi's, I know that first trimester miscarriages are common because the immune system goes TH1 dominant in the first half of pregnancy and if you're already TH1 dom, which so many Hashi's ladies are, it attacks the fetus (not to mention that some Hashi's women also have antiphospholipid syndrome, which attacks the fetus). You then switch to TH2 dom. toward the second half and back to TH1, postnatal (which is why so many women go Hashi's right after pregnancy).

My acupuncturist could tell by my pulses that I'd quickly miscarry were I to get pregnant and she didn't even know I had Hashi's (I have no idea how she can tell this stuff, but she's a genius at reading pulses in regards to fertility!)

I need to start charting my temps again because my cycle is massively shifting around right now (I broke my good thermometer awhile back and need to order a new one!) I was often not ovulating most of the time in years past and I also had/have LPD. If I do decide to have a baby, I'll be closely monitoring my TH1/TH2 cytokines throughout and after the pregnancy to make sure the Hashi's stays nice and calm.

### L8F — 2012-03-25T16:26:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-5768>

Hazy this is great stuff. When I see my new funct. MD on the 5th, I will ask her to run the TH1/TH2 cytokines. I do not think I have the antiphospholipid thing cause they run those tests with the initial fertility evals. Thank goodness--anticardiolipin negative.

My whole maternal line had post natal thyroiditis, mother and GM with thyroidectomies. Others with goiters. I think that post early miscarriage, that is when stuff really went south for me. Physical stress of hormones trying to ramp up without the raw materials, plus the major emotional upheaval of joy joy pregnancy to sad sad pregnancy loss. Fatness, hair loss, non-depressed lethargy all followed. My body never bounced back.

Can't even believe that I am only learning this stuff in the last 5 months or so. Thankfully all of you informational fonts are out there having gone ahead of me with Hashi's and other health problems I never knew I had!! That is one of the weirdest things, thinking you are super healthy, and then figuring out that you are the opposite. Interesting also is that during my intern year (post break up with now husband, and general intern stress) I lost half of my hair volume. Saw a specialist in hair loss, who diagnosed telogen effluvium, and offered Rogaine if I wanted. Jeez. I think my TSH was totally normal or "good" so thyroid was ruled out.

I also find it generally fascinating that for some people this pattern of LR/poor health manifests as energy stuff, others GI dysfunction, others infertility, and still others random other stuff. We are so individual.

Hazy, do start charting again, cause I would love for us to all to have additional data sources for ourselves to match up with symptoms, and lab work. I want to get all geeked out on this, and hope to use charting a little more effectively for BHRT support stuff for ourselves if needed.

Part of the problem I see with a lot of the charting is that there is no great source that I have found that connects charts with symptoms, labs etc. It is all guesswork on sites that I have found like "That drop in your chart 5 DPO is from X, and that other thing is from Y I think. Or, "ugly charts don't mean anything, plenty of people have them".

### L8F — 2012-03-25T20:01:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-5909>

> **MamaGrok;5869 wrote:** I don't remember hearing of these; would this be an effective way to confirm or rule out Hashi's? What are these labs called?

I think the list is somewhere in Chris Kresser's stuff, and I just found it in Datis Kharrazian's book:

quote:

Too much NK and cytotoxic T cell activity=TH-1 dom.

Too much B cell activity=TH-2 dominant

TH1 cytokines: IL-2, IL-12, TNFalpha, Interferon

TH2 cytokines: IL-4, IL-13, IL-10

He describes 90% Hashi's being TH1 dominant. I know Dr. K is not a fan of this book, and previously mentioned it was because of some flawed assumptions (not sure what those are, as this book has many areas)

I have not looked at Kressers stuff too too much, but I believe he is a really good clinician and knows his research. He has a cascade he tests I believe, and can't remember where I saw it...

### hazyjane — 2012-03-25T20:56:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-5936>

> **L8F;5943 wrote:** I think the list is somewhere in Chris Kresser's stuff, and I just found it in Datis Kharrazian's book:
>
> quote:
>
> Too much NK and cytotoxic T cell activity=TH-1 dom.
>
> Too much B cell activity=TH-2 dominant
>
>
>
> TH1 cytokines: IL-2, IL-12, TNFalpha, Interferon
>
>
>
> TH2 cytokines: IL-4, IL-13, IL-10
>
>
>
> He describes 90% Hashi's being TH1 dominant. I know Dr. K is not a fan of this book, and previously mentioned it was because of some flawed assumptions (not sure what those are, as this book has many areas)
>
> I have not looked at Kressers stuff too too much, but I believe he is a really good clinician and knows his research. He has a cascade he tests I believe, and can't remember where I saw it...

The CD4/CD8 panel tests the cytokine balance (Direct Labs carries the Apex CD4/CD8 panels)

This is helpful since TPO antibodies can fluctuate quite a lot and even be negative, since, like Chris said, most Hashi's (though not all) is TH1 dominant and often the B cell/antibody production is so suppressed by the T cells that you get low antibody readings. That's why "negative" TPOab results don't always rule out Hashi's. Also, getting the antibody test done when you've been gluten-free for awhile will also often produce low/negative results.

I wanted Dr. K to explain what it was that he disagreed with in Dr. Kharrazian's book. I disagree with some of the diet stuff (like eating frequently and his method for insulin resistance) but the immune system stuff has been a lifesaver for me.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-26T20:23:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-6468>

> **L8F;6205 wrote:** That will be interesting to see the lab comparison. Also the charts. (plug here for charting)
>
>
>
> Ugh, I love my func. med lab book, but it lacks hormones etc. Do you have a good reference text?
>
> On another cycle note, I have noticed a strong aversion to the cold baths in luteal phase. I mean _strong_. I just turn into a mess when thinking about getting in, then think it out like this: Stress-->cortisol--->won't stay in if head not right--->don't stress--->don't get in--->feel guilt and stress anyway. I think it is clear after two luteal phases that this is a pattern. I think others have had a harder time in LP with cold???

On the CT aversion, try not to stress about it. Your body will tell you how much of it you can take at a time. Being adverse in the LP, may mean that your body has enough to deal with at the moment without going on a toxin cleanse. I haven't had too much time to reread all the CT series again and again( yet), BUT I know that getting cold adapted overnight isn't necessarily going to be the best goal for everyone. Sure CT is a help, but the mayhem is not to be ignored either. While I know that anyone TTC hears clocks ticking 24/7, everyone and I mean everyone who is trying to get Optimal needs to devote some of that mindfulness time to thinking about where we are right now.

When my little guy was diagnosed with a developmental disorder a wise geneticist said this about his prognosis... Look back at the progress over the last 6-12 mos. What do you see? Are things improving? Slowly? Explosively? Leveling off? That is the type of progress you can then forecast for the next 6-12 mos. Those have been very wise words that have kept me positive about progress and woke me up to how I was the one who wasn't dong as well this year as last year.

So Look back 1 year, 6mos, 3 mos. Look where you are now. Then look ahead the same distances and visualize change. Natural healing is slower, but more permanent. Revel for a moment about how much better we all are just for the knowledge we have now that wasn't even available a year ago!

So if it takes until the next real natural winter to get cold-adapted, I am going to try and accept that. And when I'm downright afraid to get in the tub because I just got over a sinus infection that made me want to rip my teeth out, well I'm going to give myself a break and remember that CT isn't the be all/end all of human existence. There are plenty of other parts of the optimal puzzle to work on.

The other thing I would add is a bit of corollary to my LP weight loss philosophy. Since I generally gain a few pounds of water weight with the whole PMS/DD episode each month, I try to look at the scale and tell myself - "no gain right now, is actually a loss" The whole PMS masks the underlying good things my body is doing to lose weight and get healthy. So while LP might keep someone from CT, or add water, break out, or all the other joys of hormones... it is just a mask. If we are really working on the sleep, the food, the right exercise, goodness is happening deep within that we cannot often see.

So, NO STRESS! Stress is useless unless there is a catastrophe in progress that you can avert.

There are hormone reference ranges listed in Dr. Kharrazian's book.

I totally know what you mean about sometimes being repelled by CT. That hasn't happened to me recently, but when I was releasing more toxins and having more days with not a lot of energy and/or crappy sleep, I could just sense that sometimes it wasn't the right time. I noticed I didn't recover as fast and couldn't warm up when I ignored my intuition.

Now I'm back to craving the cool water. I still have tired days here and there and a touch of am adrenal fatigue (which I just got back on my chiro's special herb blend for), but overall, I'm doing well with it.

Here's a weird thought: right now the sun is in a super-active sun spot cycle and we've been having a lot of geomagnetic storms from solar flares (these can interupt electronics, etc). Since humans are electrical beings too (esp, our brains!) I just really wonder how these storms affect our_ bodies??

I'm fairly sensitive to EMFs and such, so I wonder if some of my intermittent tiredness could be linked to solar storms? I notice that it feels ridiculously good to lay on the earth and get electrically grounded.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-03-27T02:51:49+0300

For awhile, I went -- thinking the little amount wouldn't hurt me. But, after finding out that I'm allergic to both gluten and yeast (alcohol) through muscle testing, I just don't go up for communion anymore. It's kind of strange not to be able to participate in this part of my faith anymore, but I'm hoping God will understand.

### MamaGrok — 2012-03-27T04:19:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-6751>

> **Carolina;6689 wrote:** Those of you who are Catholic - what do you do when it comes to communion during mass?
>
> In other words, gluten and/or alcohol?

I don't worry about it at all from a paleo standpoint ... in other words, if I didn't have specific sensitivities, I'd receive and not think twice about it. And I did, for a long time. In fact, the "accidents of bread" were the only gluten-like exposure I had on any kind of regular basis for a very long time, yet when I had tests done, I still showed very strong signs of gluten intolerance. I think that it wouldn't have been a problem if I was starting with a healthy gut ... but I think we all know that wasn't the case!

(I think that some day when I find myself with extraneous energy again, I'll write to some nuns who make communion hosts and request they start procuring organic spelt instead. I'd fund their whole operation if they did! Hey, why not sprouted spelt, while we're at it ... since they can't let it sour (has to be unleavened).)

So I looked into the options, which include the "low-gluten" host (said to have so little that it can't possibly hurt a Celiac, but I don't see any reason to chance it right now), receiving from the chalice only (if you have Celiac, you would have to be first in line and make sure it's not the chalice used by the priest, which has had the Host commingled in it), and doing a spiritual communion.

I choose the chalice only. My tricky part is that I'm always up singing in the balcony and they bring only the Host up there, so I have to go downstairs and then back up between the two songs. That's usually fine, b/c usually I'm either not singing on the end of the first or the beginning of the second, but sometimes I have to do both, and won't have time, so then I do a [spiritual](http://http://www.ourcatholicprayers.com/spiritual-communion.html) [communion](http://http://www.ewtn.com/devotionals/prayers/blsac4.htm), and don't worry about it a moment. In fact, I like to make spiritual communions often, every day, several times a day if I can!

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-03-27T14:46:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-6927>

> **PaleoCowgirl;6747 wrote:** For awhile, I went -- thinking the little amount wouldn't hurt me. But, after finding out that I'm allergic to both gluten and yeast (alcohol) through muscle testing, I just don't go up for communion anymore. It's kind of strange not to be able to participate in this part of my faith anymore, but I'm hoping God will understand.

First I would go down the road of healing yourself. Many, many AI situations are not permanent. If you haven't been diagnosed with a genetic switch that has been thrown, it may not be permanent. From my practical experience, I would say your ability to take communion under both species isn't necessarily lost forever. Receiving the Precious Blood would be my focus first if I were ill. It appears to me that ridding oneself of yeast issues is much easier than gluten ones.

There are rules about the content of the matter of all the sacraments. Here are those that apply to the Eucharist [http://www.cuf.org/faithfacts/details\_view.asp?ffID=45](http://http://www.cuf.org/faithfacts/details_view.asp?ffID=45)

When you feel you may be ready to receive again, make an appointment to talk with a deacon or priest in your parish. I don't think you need to show up with a "note from your doctor", but knowing the requirements of the Church, and being able to talk in an informed manner about the situation will go far with the clergy. Frame it from the understanding that you have a strong desire to receive but recognize that there are constraints on the situation from your health and the logistics of distributing Communion. And make sure you mention that you know God never asks the impossible... just the difficult. That pretty much can't be argued with by anyone.

To take MamaGrok's comments about spiritual communion a bit further. Remember, the Eucharist is a sacrament... outward sign of inward grace, instituted by Christ... (Baltimore Catechism anyone?). The sacraments are there for us to confer sacramental grace, and and sanctifying grace - that which makes us holy and ultimately creatures worthy of Heaven. WE are bound by the sacraments - we can receive grace from them when we receive them properly. But God who is omni - everything - omnipotent, omniscient, etc. is NOT bound by the Sacraments. He can confer grace however He wishes, whenever He wishes. At the moment the ecstasy of Teresa of Avila comes to mind!! Or Margaret Mary Alcoque in adoration of the Blessed Sacrament. So, if the desire is to receive grace, He knows that and He will make it available to you by whatever means. Continue to receive the other sacraments worthily as appropriate. Pray upon it... plead like Hannah in the OT. Focus on making the most of the corporal form that is the body God gave you. Make it as healthy as possible - not for vanity, or easing of the cross you carry, but for the greater glory of He who made you. And I would offer reading "In the School of the Holy Spirit" by Jacques Philippe. It will help you focus on being present to all the other actions of grace in your life. He is truly present in the Real Presence, but receiving the Real Presence is not the only action of His grace.

ad maioram Dei gloriam - cgk

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-03-27T15:37:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-6945>

> **cgk;4938 wrote: I don't have a whit of experience with Hashi's so I'm learning it all from women out here, but Jennifer I think you are doing the right thing trying to heal this all naturally. That said, I would put the LRx in ahead of CT unless you feel your diet has been really good for a while. CT does seem to bring out the best and the worst all at the same time. If the diet is dialed in better, you might have less hormonal bumps going down the CT road.
>
>
>
> I wouldn't throw in the towel and admit early onset menopause yet by any means. I've seen too much improvement to my own situation - badly bumping along through perimenopause - with getting LS, that I think it merits giving this a try. That all said, I think of LR as an over arching problem that masks other issues and compounds them. So taking it off the table by getting LS will help see what else might be genuinely broken and need help with hormonal intervention or some other therapy.
>
>
>
> There is a website called womentowomen.com that is a group in Maine that tries to work these issues naturally. They sell supplements which always gives me pause - particularly since they aren't custom blended for each patient, but a group of different "formulas" They also shun ketogenic diets. That all said, they do have quite a bit of good reading on thyroid/adrenal/sex hormone relationships that seems to be legit. They do have some recent articles about early onset menopause, that might be worth reading - if nothing else to see if you genuinely fit the profile or if there is something that just needs fixing.
>
>
>
> Yay! Buffalo... I'm originally from Rochester. Nice long natural CT season up there!!**

**

Hi CGK and thanks! Just seeing your post now. I'm getting frustrated that I'm not seeing any results. My morning temps are very close to 97 and my TSH is in range but higher than I'd like (2 something). Rather than going through the both of having my med adjusted (dr appointment, explaining myself, follow up bloodwork - blah), I was hoping that CT would kick in and I wouldn't need an increase! But now that the more natural Buffalo weather has returned, I am finding it really hard to do CT - basically I'm already freezing and it's hard to talk myself into the tub. Had a bad time of it yesterday because I forgot about this deadline on an on-line project, so I didn't finish that until after 9 pm and didn't finish my cold tub until 10pm. It's so hard for me to sleep when I'm so cold. I keep my heat on 55 as well, so my bedroom was freezing! I finally broke down and got out a space heater. My room temp was 52 degrees. I left the heater on for a good 45 minutes and then turned it off to sleep. But I woke up at 1:30 am freezing and having to pee and I turned it back on. I woke up at 5:30 am and the heater was still on. Said it was 66 in my room and my temp was 97.3 - the highest I have ever seen it! I try not to turn on heat sources after CT because I feel like I'm probably "undoing" the cold adapting but it's so hard to be cold ALL THE TIME. I am frustrated that I've been at CT for over two weeks and am no where near cold adapted. And now I'm reading that Dr K says 30 minutes isn't long enough. I can barely stand 35 minutes in 55 degree water! BRRR My poor fingers and toes were white as ice yesterday.

L8F;5943 wrote:** I think the list is somewhere in Chris Kresser's stuff, and I just found it in Datis Kharrazian's book:

quote:

Too much NK and cytotoxic T cell activity=TH-1 dom.

Too much B cell activity=TH-2 dominant

TH1 cytokines: IL-2, IL-12, TNFalpha, Interferon

TH2 cytokines: IL-4, IL-13, IL-10

He describes 90% Hashi's being TH1 dominant. I know Dr. K is not a fan of this book, and previously mentioned it was because of some flawed assumptions (not sure what those are, as this book has many areas)

I have not looked at Kressers stuff too too much, but I believe he is a really good clinician and knows his research. He has a cascade he tests I believe, and can't remember where I saw it...

I would be interested to hear why Dr K doesn't like Dr Kharrazian's book. I listened to a few podcasts with Dr Kharrazian and purchased the book (haven't read it yet- so many books to read!). I thought I might be Th2 dominant but after some muscle testing with an accupuncturist, looks like Th1 dom for sure. Of course, I was kind of happy because the recommended things for Th1 dom are things I like - coffee, chocolate, red wine! Too bad I am avoiding all these things for now on Leptin Reset and Adrenal protocol!

I am frustrated that I've been at CT for over two weeks and am no where near cold adapted. And now I'm reading that Dr K says 30 minutes isn't long enough. I can barely stand 35 minutes in 55 degree water! BRRR My poor fingers and toes were white as ice yesterday.

Okay, white toes and fingers is a BIG no-no with CT. That is a sign that core temp is dropping, which it should not be. I have only done CT at night ONCE and was also miserably cold the whole time I was sleeping... BUT I woke up sick the next day - the first of 4 days of fever at 103+

When I've done CT - been off 2 weeks now, I did it in the morning after I cleared out the kids to school. I realize that may not be an option for others, but it would sound like breakfast+ cold shower or bath as a better option right now. If you aren't sleeping, you are going to just rattle the hormonal cascade.

From my readings of Dr. K's blog, fixing Leptin doesn't necessarily cure everything, but it does take that over arching problem off the table. It is quite possible from what I understand to become LS and still have thyroid or adrenal issues that need to be managed in and off themselves. The degree to which CT can fix them is definitely going to be proportional to the degree than you can even use them. IMHO, there is nothing wrong with using the CW/standard western med pharmaceuticals to get things in balance and then try to wean off of them, using CT or other interventions.

I have to wonder if those of us who are suffering a bit in the chart or other ways from CT aren't just going at it too hard, too fast. I had 47 years to get my body this out of whack. I started seriously fixing it 4 years ago. It has taken me 3 years and 10 mos of that just to isolate SOME of the problems. CT is definitely a powerful fix for many things... but it is renovation with a pretty hard core demolition phase. I for one am sticking to the LRx and post reset protocol primarily as a means to get my house in better order before I start nuking it with more CT - for example eating more seasonally, finding my sweet spot level of carbs, letting the supplements I've taken have a good 3-6 mos to right some of the wrongs. As I've said in another posts, there are many, many parts of optimal to work on. No one cleans their house by doing every room at once. It might be best to ponder which order to do things - particularly if you are working with a known health issue and pharmaceuticals.

My chart this month is still running below my average temps for the last 54 cycles. So the LRx and the little bit of CT I've done have definitely changed my BBT. Will have to wait until next cycle when I am hopefully fever/antibiotics free to see if the LS state really does continue to have me run colder.

### thyme — 2012-03-27T19:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-7198>

Jennifer, are you CTing after you eat? Say, half an hour to an hour after your meal? For me that makes a \_huge\_ difference. The last two nights after dinner, I've stripped down and jumped into a cold (43F) shower because dinner made my body \_hot\_ in 40 degree weather, but even before that whackiness, I found the CT thing much easier after a big meal.

Also--I adapted to CT a lot faster as soon as I went slower. My tap water is 43 F; after all baths at that temperature, I was miserable afterwards, and I couldn't fathom how the rest of y'all could be adding ice to that water. When I brought my bath up to 55F, I could sit for an hour or so, not shiver at all afterwards, and feel super-energized for the day...and no more cold fingers & toes. When I was used to that temp, I started turning things down and now, well, my cold adaptation seems to be running fine.\* I guess that is to say--I'd echo the start slow thought, with water as "warm" as you need to not feel miserable. That will speed up your adaptation.

\*although there could be alternative hormonal strangeness at work there...per the BBT thing, I'm now 2 days late, have three days of full ferning on the microscope, and the pee sticks are still negative. How much longer am I supposed to be non-cortisol about this????

### barattafam@gmail.com — 2012-03-28T04:42:00+0300

> **MamaGrok;6787 wrote:** I don't worry about it at all from a paleo standpoint ... in other words, if I didn't have specific sensitivities, I'd receive and not think twice about it. And I did, for a long time. In fact, the "accidents of bread" were the only gluten-like exposure I had on any kind of regular basis for a very long time, yet when I had tests done, I still showed very strong signs of gluten intolerance. I think that it wouldn't have been a problem if I was starting with a healthy gut ... but I think we all know that wasn't the case!
>
>
>
> (I think that some day when I find myself with extraneous energy again, I'll write to some nuns who make communion hosts and request they start procuring organic spelt instead. I'd fund their whole operation if they did! Hey, why not sprouted spelt, while we're at it ... since they can't let it sour (has to be unleavened).)
>
>
>
> So I looked into the options, which include the "low-gluten" host (said to have so little that it can't possibly hurt a Celiac, but I don't see any reason to chance it right now), receiving from the chalice only (if you have Celiac, you would have to be first in line and make sure it's not the chalice used by the priest, which has had the Host commingled in it), and doing a spiritual communion.
>
>
>
> I choose the chalice only. My tricky part is that I'm always up singing in the balcony and they bring only the Host up there, so I have to go downstairs and then back up between the two songs. That's usually fine, b/c usually I'm either not singing on the end of the first or the beginning of the second, but sometimes I have to do both, and won't have time, so then I do a [spiritual](http://http://www.ourcatholicprayers.com/spiritual-communion.html) [communion](http://http://www.ewtn.com/devotionals/prayers/blsac4.htm), and don't worry about it a moment. In fact, I like to make spiritual communions often, every day, several times a day if I can!

Good points. For some reason, our parish only offers the chalice on feast days. I really don't know why!

I've been more concerned with gluten lately due to my Hashi's, and am trying to figure out for myself if I need to avoid even the small amount in the Host. No option for low gluten here, either. But what a fantastic idea spiritual communion is!

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-28T18:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-7757>

Lucy.

After the past couple of sleepless nights I really WANT to avoid CT today. Not sure if it's cycle related or not. But I didn't sleep from 1-3 am last night. Doesn't help that ds was up during that time frame as well and wouldn't go back to sleep unless I cuddled him!!!

I think some of the aversion is related to the sleep issue & what ever crap my body is trying to get rid of. It seems like its my body rebelling at the thought of CT not my mind b/c when I miss a session I crave it

Everything is discombobulated right now. Hormones are insane (as evident by the migraine I had for the duration of totm & the one I got last night). Add that to what ever crap I'm eliminating and no wonder why I feel out!

Going to the chiro this afternoon after work for an adjustment (taking the kids too!)

Given all the above once the water got up to my waist a serenity settled around me. I actually pushed the CT today and got the H2O down to close to 50 degrees & used 2 compression shirts to cover the remainder of my torso.

What's crazy? I really love the numbness tingling across my trunk & lower body. No shivering. Wonder if I will after the fact. B/c for some reason I don shiver at the beginning of CT just at the end of a long session greater than 1hr & 30 min

Any clues to this????

### L8F — 2012-03-28T21:03:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-7858>

I am not sure. Total body hormonal detox upheaval?? I suspect there _has_ to be a huge hormonal component. LP aversion is like clockwork for me, the last two cycles. I think clearly the migraine issue sucks, --and as you know--, is directly hormonal. I wish I had a better answer, but I think I am going to start adding cold aversion to my charting and see. Now that I am looking for it though, not sure what that might do. Where are you cycle wise?

On another note, my food cravings are out of control, and I have never noticed them this bad before. It started with candied ginger in bulk one day, then it just kept going despite detrimental side effects like brain fog and bloating and hangover feelings. I just ate a POUND of sea scallops with ghee, and 3/4 of a chocolate bar, 65 percent, as well as one date. WTF?? PMS?

Last PM I was also WIDE awake (new for me) from 230 for about two hours. I had had coconut based faux ice cream on an idiotic whim, and didn't sleep. I haven't gotten into a bath in two weeks since being sick from likely food poisoning etc. UGH!!! The sleep for me is carb related I think, though could be detox. Sounds like from other areas of the forum that sleep really is really a problem for a lot of people. At work I see a TON of sleep issues crop up around change to Spring...unclear why, but likely some circadian piece, right?

I think the BHRT is great, and want to do it, but it throws a huge number of variables in. Wonder if charting temps might be more data points for you to look at, or just might be way too much with what you've got going on in AMs with 2 little kids etc. I think you sound pretty nicely cold adapted, and I imagine a big detox with the meds, cold and hormones all processing in your poor body!

I am letting myself off the hook a little with the baths until I feel more ready. I wish that I will have your same outcome of getting in and then relaxing. Not sure I can get there right now. I am freezing in the house 'cause it is freezing here, and I am doing T shirt only walking the dog etc, so some cold...

Rambling way to say, I think you can push thru and just hit it hard and ee if things improve, or back off one area and see if anything improves. Test out lightening up something a bit and see??? The problem is without sleep, it is hard to feel good.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-03-28T22:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-7922>

> **Shijin13;7799 wrote:** . But I didn't sleep from 1-3 am last night. Doesn't help that ds was up during that time frame as well and wouldn't go back to sleep unless I cuddled him!!!What's crazy? I really love the numbness tingling across my trunk & lower body. No shivering. Wonder if I will after the fact. B/c for some reason I don shiver at the beginning of CT just at the end of a long session greater than 1hr & 30 min
>
>
>
> Any clues to this????

Shijin, I've been waking up at 1:30 (or 2:30 if I go to bed a little later) feeling hot and I have to pee. I re-read CT7 and most of it is over my head, but Dr K does talk about the different things that happen with leptin around that time. I also see in the comments that he mentions that he has been doing some kind of extreme CT that has left his toro numb. So, it's interesting that you brought up that feeling. Things that make you go hmm....Here is the section of his blog that I thought might apply to 1-3am. Maybe you can figure it out?

15\. At midnight leptin begins to enter the hypothalamus to bind to its receptor in the hypothalamus to signal energy reserves while also yoking energy metabolism to sleep via the hypocretin neurons that control all the sleep cycles. In diurnal animals, sleepiness occurs as the circadian element causes the release of the hormone melatonin and a gradual decrease in core body temperature. This timing is affected by one’s chronotype.

16\. It is the circadian rhythm that determines the ideal timing of a correctly structured and restorative sleep episode. Melatonin, the hormone from the pineal gland, called the “darkness hormone ” is of great importance in the functioning of the SCN. The most important target of melatonin in humans appears to be the SCN, as the SCN contains the highest density for melatonin receptors. A double effect of melatonin in the SCN, namely, an immediate effect and long term effect, has encouraged its worldwide use against the ill effects of jet lag. As an immediate effect, melatonin is found to suppress neuronal SCN activity towards night time levels. In terms of long term effect, melatonin can phase shift and amplify circadian rhythmicity of the SCN. Melatonin application has been found to be useful in synchronizing the endogenous circadian rhythms not only in people who suffer from jet lag, but also in blind individuals, patients with dementia, and in shift workers. With seasonal changes in night duration, there are parallel changes in the duration of melatonin secretion, and this leads to more secretion in winter than as compared to summer. In the cold environments of fall and winter, melatonin couples to eNOS and not to light levels. In warm adapted humans in the tropics light remains the focus of SCN entrainment year round.

17\. After the 4 hours of darkness, melatonin secretion increases and this allows plasma leptin to enter the hypothalamus if we are sensitive to its receptor. If we are leptin resistant, this process can no longer occur.

18\. Once leptin enters and binds to its receptors, it effects the lateral hypothalamic tracts to immediately send a second messenger signal to the thyroid to signal it to up-regulate thyroid function and efficiency. This specifically is how we can raise our basal metabolic rate when we are leptin sensitive. These coupled events, matched with leptinÃ¢€™s actions peripherally in muscles, occur at the UCP3 sitesÃ¢€Â¦Ã¢€Â¦to burn fat as we sleep at a higher basal metabolic rate. This means electron chain transport does not make ATP as usual. When leptin allows this uncoupling to occur we make heat and not energy from normal metabolism. This means we will burn off our excess calories as pure heat. This is one reason why calories in and calories out argument makes no biologic sense once you understand how leptin works. Humans are built to burn fat at night as we sleep to loose excess weight we donÃ¢€™t need.

19\. **The timing of the leptin action is also critical. It usually occurs between 12-3 AM and is tied to when you last ate and how much darkness your retina (SCN) have seen. This generally occurs soon after our hypothalamus releases another hormone called prolactin from our pituitary gland in the brain.**20\. The surge of Prolactin is normally quite large in normal darkness but is significantly diminished in artificially lit environments after sunset. This was shown in the CT 2 video. This has big implications for modern humans. The reason is that prolactin release is coordinated with sleep cycles where autophagy is at its highest efficiency and where Growth Hormone is released. If this is diminished we generally see lower DHEA levels clinically and higher IL-6 levels on cytokine arrays. This is a measure of uncoupling of sleep from normal metabolism. I base every bio hack I do on this step in circadian biology because it is the most important.

21\. The normal large circadian prolactin surge we Ã¢€œshould seeÃ¢€Â￾ at around midnight, after leptin enters the brain, does not happen if the patient has leptin resistance, sleep apnea, or has eaten food too close (within 3-4 hours) to bedtime. This blocks leptinÃ¢€™s ability to enter the brain because insulin spikes. As mentioned above, this step is usually impaired if you are a post menopausal female as well. This is often why older women sleep badly and gain weight they can not seem to lose in the gym even with a good paleo template and good habits. This is another reason I am a big advocate for bio identical hormone optimization in women. This need is greatest in women who are warm adapted. The need is lowest in the cold adapted females because their leptin levels are already low due to the cold. Post menopausal women who are cold adapted tend to do amazingly well clinically in most disease parameters in my clinical experience. The main problem they face is that their vanity and dogma keep them from using the cold pathways to become rockstars as they age. Exercise training tends to frustrate post menopausal women because if their hormone response is altered they have a lot of trouble as they age. Men on the other hand do not lose their GH levels until 50-55 years old usually. They are also protected by their testosterone levels which persist throughout life provided that they are not suffering from inflammation which directly lowers their free and total testosterone levels. GH and testosterone keep a manÃ¢€™s heart and muscles in tip top shape. If inflammation destroys these levels earlier in life it can show up even in younger people. I am finding this clinical result is an epidemic in my own practice.

Wine with dinner (more carbs ) But after two nights not sleeping/worrying about it/role playing it in my head... I slept like a stone last night. So some of my PMS may be the sleep. ### Caroline Cooper — 2012-03-31T21:47:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-9340>

> **L8F;2771 wrote: Hi, thought it would be nice to have a thread here for those women who have cycle questions, issues and concerns, and to not drag the poor guys along for the ride. Anyway, I have been charting (obsessively, I might add) for many years, and know a lot about it. I have noticed that two months into the LR/Post LRx that I did not have to take OTC drugs for cramps. This was HUGE--first time in 20 years. Also noted temps were very very steady for much of cycle, moving right toward textbook chart. This month I ovulated right at day 13/14, which is traditionally the "right" time to ovulate, but many don't. I was running at day 21 at the earliest previously.
>
>
>
> For those who don't have a clue what I am talking about, taking your temp first thing in the AM after waking, before talking or moving etc, is a great way to see what is happening in your cycle. It can give you (free) info on Estrogen and Progesterone levels, as well as whether or not you are ovulating or not. All you need is to have at least one ovary and it should work just fine. No uterus needed! I find
>
> **

**

Hi Friends,

I was really worried about this too. I have been doing the SCD/GAPS/modified paleo diet for over six years. I found I did better on a low carbohydrate version of the diet though I have never tried to be ketogenic. I have also played around with seasonal eating. I have cured myself of asthma, allergies, chronic sinus infections, yeast infections, osteoarthritis and epilepsy.

BUT I do have a low body temperature. I measure my morning temperature before rising and have done so for over 35 cycles. My charts would say I am estrogen dominant and I have a standard infertility pattern. (I am 46.) My charts indicate that I have an adrenal problem not a thyroid problem due to my extreme daily fluctuations in my morning temperatures. If any of you ladies that have done charting for a long time have any observations about what I have just said, please comment, I would appreciate the feedback.

**

There are two main views out there about low body temperature. One says its a sign of endocrine dysfunction. This appears to be the dominant view in the paleo community at this time with all the talk about safe starches. The other view would say, I am in calorie restriction, and that lower body temperature is great because it will increase my longevity.

I have been trying to figure out which of these two opinions might be happening for me. I have been experimenting with over-eating, safe starches, adrenal extracts, herbals, etc from about 2009. None of these experiments have increased my body temperature for any length of time. I was starting to think that maybe there was nothing wrong with a low body temperature since I appear healthy in other ways.

It was late last year that I learned about Dr Krase's work but I really didn't understand his earlier posts. It has been just recently that I have come back to his website to find the cold thermogenesis massives. Wow, what can I say. His material blew my mind. If he is right, and he has done six years of clinic work, it doesn't matter if my adrenals are burnt-out or my thyroid is under-functioning. Cold thermogenesis, a seasonal ketogenic diet and balancing circadian cycles will open a pathway in the brain that will overrun any glandular endocrine dysfunction.

By the way, I asked Dr Kruse about his opinion on low body temperature. He thinks it's better but he didn't give a reason. Maybe he will expand on the reason why here.

I have gone moderately ketogenic. I am turning off the lights at sunset and I am in the first stages of cold thermogensis. I will keep you posted as to how it goes and what it does to my system. I like the term, biohack!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-03-31T23:24:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-9389>

> **L8F;9411 wrote: Tell me more, what does your pattern look like? I am not too sure what E dom looks like on a chart, and want to know. Thanks for posting.
>
>
> **L8F;9411 wrote:** Tell me more, what does your pattern look like? I am not too sure what E dom looks like on a chart, and want to know. Thanks for posting.**

**

Hi L8F,

In Katie Singer's book The Garden of Fertility** (pg.112) she states signals that point to low progestrone levels: _"temperatures zigzags up and down around ovulation, the luteal phase lasts eleven days or less, temperatures dip onto or below coverlines during the luteal phase"_, etc.

I have all of these problems pointing to low progestrone, thus estrogen dominance. My normal charts are up-and-down low before ovulation, a weak transition during ovulation usually taking two days, and a up-and-down post-ovulation. I would guess the standard infertility cycle. I have low temperatures throughout my cycle which would normally point to thyroid dysfunction. But Katie states somewhat in the book that low stable temperatures mean thyroid problems and low fluctuating temperatures point towards adrenal problems.

Cold thermogenesis may be changing this pattern but it is too early to say for sure.

Hi cgk,

I know what you mean. Thinking that low body temperature isn't a problem is a paradigm shift even when I know that there are alternative views on body temperature. Well, I have spent over two years experimenting with trying to shift my temperature without any long term success. Accepting the low temperature and moving forward seems like a reasonable move at this point. "Thinking" that it is "healthy" rather than "a problem" will have a positive effect on a mind level.

Katie Singer's book **The Garden of Fertility** has a section on using charting to gauge gynecological health. I found this section particularly illuminating. She also has a section on how to improve health with dietary changes, she follows Weston A Price Foundation food guidelines, and a section on called "lunaception" about night lighting and night darkness for conception. I have been thinking about light for a long time and wondering about its positive and negative effects on people. Another book called Lights Out: Sleep, Sugar, and Survival** by TS Wiley also talks about light problems. I am not on-board with her hormone program though.

I'm coming off HBC in February and would like to get back to my regular cycle -- unfortunately, between the estrogen dump of CT, my hypothyroid/hashis issues and adrenal fatigue, my temps are all over the place, and I feel like I'm a long way from ovulating once again.

I'm wondering if I should continue to progesterone or if it's competing against estrogen. I know I need an estrogen build-up to menstruate...maybe I'm off base. Any advice would be appreciated.

### L8F — 2012-04-04T16:08:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-11248>

I am not too sure about the P cream at this time, but I just looked at your chart again (saved the email link, hope you don't mind) and still in the same boat with anovulatory cycles. I forget what your labs were in terms of E and P numbers...Can you remind me? Also usually the OTC P cream is low dose anyway. Some docs like a baseline level of P thru cycle, others don't. Not sure who to believe...

The other theory with P is that you can create a false cycle with it by using it cyclicly, and stopping at a certain point to stimulate the natural drop in P that signals end of cycle, and MAYBE that might help the body figure it out. I am not sure this would be effective with OTC doses, or at all. I have not heard of anyone doing it to re-start a cycle from anovulation.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-04-04T17:36:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-who-chart-basal-body-temps-etc.291/post-11283>

> **PaleoCowgirl;11310 wrote:** Question about progesterone. I was recommended by my naturopath to use the P cream, but since I don't have a period, I don't know when to take it, so I have been dosing every day. I'm coming off HBC in February and would like to get back to my regular cycle -- unfortunately, between the estrogen dump of CT, my hypothyroid/hashis issues and adrenal fatigue, my temps are all over the place, and I feel like I'm a long way from ovulating once again.
>
>
>
> I'm wondering if I should continue to progesterone or if it's competing against estrogen. I know I need an estrogen build-up to menstruate...maybe I'm off base. Any advice would be appreciated.

I was on bio-identical progesterone DHEA about five years ago when I last had regular doctor's care. When my doctor realized I had adrenal fatigue, he pulled me off all progesterone and I stopped DHEA. His reasoning was that giving someone progesterone and DHEA when they has adrenal fatigue was like treating pollution down-river when the cause was up-the-river. You likely have a problem with your hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis (HPA or HTPA). Again, if Dr Kruse is right, CT and seasonal ketogenic diet will open the new pathway in the hypothalamic, solving the down-stream problems with cycles.

Most women in North America are estrogen dominant. Some people think this is because of estrogen like substances in the environment. It sounds like cold thermogenesis will help dump this extra estrogen too. Just remember, when you go through menopause your adrenals will take over the production of hormones from the ovaries. Again, many believe adrenal fatigue is the reason why so many women in North America have such a difficult time with menopause. Protect your adrenals by reduction of stress and removal of stimulants.

I hope some of that is helpful.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-11T15:12:14+0300

Ok.. I'm a total HOT MESS of HORMONES! WTF?! I have no clue if this is DETOX, the adjustment to the hormones, the or the chaos of PMS! Last night DH and I had a total blow out, feelings were hurt on both sides, and I was so mad I left and went for a drive - which freaked DH out b/c he knew he EFFED up. Eventually I came back and we ended up talking through everything - laying both of our issues out, and trying to figure out what we each needed to do to fix what we both broke. fortunately, even though we're both bad at communication, and telling the other person what's wrong - when we have a blow out - the results are for the better of our relationship. we decided we each needed to come up w/5 things we wanted to work on for ourselves, and 5 things we want to work on for each other and the family,we'll share those w/each other and we'll focus on 1 from each list, a month for the next 5mths, then start over.

it took us talking until about 3am to get everything out on the table, and get over our anger at each other, and hurt feelings... but at least we worked it out. ugghhh the end result to all this was a freaking migraine due to lack of sleep, and all the emotional stress... And here I was doing so well w/o a migraine... so Now I've had a migraine - but I don't know if its the result of hormones (I don't think it is - based upon what happened last night) or just stress from the blow out - and the ensuing late night talk session to get it worked out...
